2.label: Undefined - needs better definition
2.def: AGI definition is uncertain and lacks widespread agreement, requiring better definition in consultation with experts
2.cite: Recognizes the significant level of uncertainty and unknowns associated with general purpose AI systems achieving AGI. At the same time, the AI Working Group recognizes that there is not widespread agreement on the definition of AGI or threshold by which it will officially be achieved.

2.label: 5-10 years
2.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

2.label: Serious risks including doomsday scenarios
2.cite: We had one on doomsday scenarios, because some people, well, they say if there's only a 10% chance they could create a virus that would wipe out humanity, ought to stop it.

2.label: Multiple risk categories requiring attention
2.cite: At the same time, we each recognized the potential risks AI could present, including altering our workforce in the short-term and long-term, raising questions about the application of existing laws in an AI-enabled world, changing the dynamics of our national security, and raising the threat of potential doomsday scenarios.

2.label: Supports compute thresholds for AI reporting
2.def: Compute-based reporting requirements using FLOP thresholds to identify high-risk AI models that may pose national security concerns.
2.cite: One of the most interesting pieces is the Biden administration invoked their wartime powers under the Defense Production Act to require developers of dual use foundation models trained at 10X26 FLOPs or higher – so just a little bit higher than the EU threshold − to report those training runs to the Department of Commerce.

2.label: Strongly supports expanding chip export controls
2.def: Export controls on semiconductor manufacturing equipment and chips to prevent adversaries like China from accessing critical technologies for military and AI development.
2.cite: The United States should not be handing the Chinese Communist Party any access to technologies that fuel their military ambitions and jeopardize U.S. national security. As China works towards global domination of the chips industry, the MATCH Act would stop the flow of advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment that China desperately needs to build up their own chip industry.

2.label: Bipartisan targeted approach
2.cite: Our Policy Roadmap also advocates for a host of regulatory recommendations that help maximize AI's potential and minimize its risks – risks like bias, and job displacement, and privacy invasion.

2.label: Targeted regulation with innovation focus
2.cite: Innovation must be our North Star. Everyone's agreed we need some kinds of guardrails, but those guardrails cannot stand in the way of innovation. Everyone agreed that any action we take on AI must encourage, not stifle, innovation.

4.label: 5-10 years
4.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

4.label: Serious risks requiring attention
4.cite: All of this is on my mind lately for a couple reasons. One is that I recently spent some time talking with AI safety researchers who I thought made a convincing case that, no matter how much time executives and regulators spend warning us about the risks here, the average person still probably hasn't grappled with them enough.

4.label: Supportive of targeted AI regulation
4.cite: One is that AI is being regulated at the right time. The government has learned its lesson from the social media era, when its near-total inattention to hyper-scaling global platforms made it vulnerable to being blindsided by the inevitable harms once they arrived.

4.label: Targeted regulation
4.cite: Writing about the Biden executive order on Monday, I argued that the industry was so far being regulated gently, and on its own terms.

7.label: Unknown
7.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

7.label: Serious risks
7.cite: If AI companies struggle to predict and mitigate relatively well-understood risks, it raises concerns about their ability to manage more complex risks.

7.label: Moderate regulation
7.cite: If AI companies struggle to predict and mitigate relatively well-understood risks, it raises concerns about their ability to manage more complex risks. While we have been encouraged by the arrival of overdue safety measures for certain chatbots, these must be accompanied by improved public self-reporting so that consumers, families, educators, and policymakers can make informed decisions around appropriate use.

7.label: Targeted regulation
7.cite: There are a bunch of things that we can't wait on including giving the government authority over catastrophic national security risk. I mean, that's something that cannot wait.

8.def: Powerful AI that is smarter than a Nobel Prize winner across most fields, can work autonomously for weeks, operates at 10x-100x human speed, and can be replicated in millions of instances - 'a country of geniuses in a datacenter'
8.cite: By powerful AI, I have in mind an AI model... In terms of pure intelligence, it is smarter than a Nobel Prize winner across most relevant fields – biology, programming, math, engineering, writing, etc.

8.label: 1-3 years
8.def: AGI as a 'country of geniuses in a data center' - millions of AI instances smarter than Nobel Prize winners, operating 10-100x faster than humans
8.cite: Within 10 years, we'll get to country of geniuses in the data center. I'm at 90% on that. With coding, I think we'll be there in one or two years.

8.label: 1-3 years timeline
8.cite: My picture for that, if you made me guess, is one to two years, maybe one to three years. It's really hard to tell. I have a strong view—99%, 95%—that all this will happen in 10 years.

8.label: 25% catastrophic risk
8.cite: Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei ups his P(doom), the risk of catastrophic existential failure to 25% in a recent interview with Axios.

8.label: Targeted regulation with red lines
8.cite: In the long run, I actually do believe that it is Congress's job. If, for example, domest-- there are possibilities with domestic mass surveillance, government buying of, you know, bulk data that has been produced on Americans, locations, personal information, political affiliation to build profiles, and it's now possible to analyze that with AI.

8.label: Targeted regulation
8.cite: I think we should look at regulating AI in the same way that cars and airplanes are regulated. Everyone realizes they have tremendous economic value, but they have to be built very carefully. If they're not created correctly, they can kill.

8.label: Targeted regulation with red lines
8.cite: However, in a narrow set of cases, we believe AI can undermine, rather than defend, democratic values. Some uses are also simply outside the bounds of what today's technology can safely and reliably do.

10.label: Unknown
10.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

10.label: Serious risks
10.cite: We're talking about a massive economic bubble. And we've seen the - depending on the exposure of that bubble, we could see 2008 style threats to economic stability.

10.label: Strongly supports compute governance through moratorium
10.def: Compute governance means federal oversight and restrictions on AI data centers and computing infrastructure until comprehensive safeguards are established.
10.cite: The bill seeks an immediate, nationwide halt on the construction or upgrading of AI data centers until Congress establishes comprehensive federal safeguards.

10.label: Strongly supports expanded export controls
10.def: Export controls on AI chips means banning U.S. exports of AI computing infrastructure to countries lacking equivalent regulatory safeguards.
10.cite: Additionally, this bill will stop a global race to see which country is the first to eliminate hundreds of millions of jobs, or the first to build an AI that destroys the planet. It accomplishes this by banning U.S. exports of AI computing infrastructure to countries that do not have safeguards in place to guarantee AI is safe and effective, workers are protected and AI does not harm the environment.

10.label: Strongly supports developer liability
10.def: Developer liability means holding AI companies legally responsible for harms caused by their systems, including civil lawsuits for victims.
10.cite: The legislation amends the Violence Against Women Act (VAWA) so that people can sue those who produce, distribute, or receive the deepfake pornography, if they "knew or recklessly disregarded" the victim did not consent to those images.

10.label: Strongly supports mandatory pre-deployment evaluations
10.def: Mandatory pre-deployment evaluations means requiring AI companies to conduct safety testing and receive government approval before releasing AI systems.
10.cite: AI is safe and effective — preventing executives in the AI industry from releasing harmful products into the world that threaten the health and well-being of working families, our privacy and civil rights and the future of humanity.

10.label: Precautionary approach
10.cite: This harm has occurred, not in spite of, but because of the absence of federal legislation to regulate AI

10.label: Precautionary approach
10.cite: Instead of legislation regarding artificial intelligence, they have been trying multiple times to pass legislation that would actually end any regulation or rulemaking, particularly on the state level, to limit any oversight of AI at all.

10.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
10.def: Federal preemption means blocking states from regulating AI through executive orders and legislation that would ban state-level AI laws.
10.cite: Instead of legislation regarding artificial intelligence, they have been trying multiple times to pass legislation that would actually end any regulation or rulemaking, particularly on the state level, to limit any oversight of AI at all.

11.label: Supports reporting requirements
11.def: Reporting requirements for advanced AI systems based on compute thresholds and capability assessments.
11.cite: Establishes an "Advanced Artificial Intelligence Evaluation Program" within the U.S. Department of Energy to evaluate advanced AI systems and collect data on the likelihood of adverse AI incidents, such as loss-of-control scenarios and weaponization by adversaries.

11.label: Strongly supports developer liability
11.def: Strict liability for AI developers for harms caused by defective design, failure to warn, and unreasonably dangerous systems.
11.cite: The Bill would establish new, broad liability frameworks for AI developers and deployers. Developers would face negligence, strict liability, and warranty-based claims for AI systems that cause harm, including property damage, physical injury, financial harm, reputational injury, or psychological anguish.

11.label: Strongly supports mandatory evaluations
11.def: Mandatory pre-deployment testing and evaluation of advanced AI systems for safety and security risks.
11.cite: Advanced AI developers that fail to participate in mandatory federal evaluations could face fines of at least $1,000,000 per day.

11.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
11.def: Federal preemption prevents states from creating their own AI laws, establishing one national standard instead of 50 discordant state ones.
11.cite: As U.S. states start to react to growing constituent concerns around the risks associated with artificial intelligence use, Tennessee Republican Sen. Marsha Blackburn said moving forward with a federal preemption standard is "imperative."

12.def: The ability of AI systems to perform all tasks that humans can perform
12.cite: We analyze how output and wages behave under different scenarios for technological progress that may culminate in Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), defined as the ability of AI systems to perform all tasks that humans can perform.

12.def: AI that possesses the ability to understand, learn, and perform any intellectual task a human being can perform
12.cite: He conjectured that artificial general intelligence (AGI)—AI that possesses the ability to understand, learn, and perform any intellectual task a human being can perform—may be realized within a span of 5 to 20 years.

12.label: 5-10 years
12.cite: Although I am highly uncertain, at the time of writing, I estimate that each of these scenarios has a greater than 10 percent probability of materializing.

12.label: 5-10 years
12.cite: I currently assign a probability of at least 10 percent to each of these scenarios, although I am highly uncertain.

12.label: Serious risk
12.cite: There are some really adverse existential risk scenarios that I will probably not have much impact on, but I hope somebody's thinking about them. If we don't, the disruption could be devastating for most of humanity.

12.label: Moderate regulation
12.cite: We need technical safety research and robust governance frameworks. We need to ensure that AI systems are aligned with human values and societal goals, not just individual or corporate objectives. This requires both technical solutions and governance structures that can handle unprecedented power concentration.

13.label: Domain-specific capability benchmark
13.def: AGI as artificial intelligence that is as capable as a human, though she considers this concept outdated given AI's mixed performance across domains
13.cite: AGI is such a funny term because many years ago it was kind of a useful concept to say when will artificial intelligence be as capable as a human. I think maybe the construct itself is now wrong—or maybe not wrong, but just outdated.

13.label: Domain-specific AGI achieved
13.def: AGI as AI that is as capable as humans in specific domains, which has already been surpassed in areas like coding
13.cite: AGI is such a funny term because many years ago it was kind of a useful concept to say when will artificial intelligence be as capable as a human. And what's interesting is by some definitions of that, we've already surpassed that.

13.label: Serious risks requiring proactive measures
13.cite: And a lot of things have to go right in order for this to not go really wrong. I think we it's our job to help them go right right as the model providers as the people that are developing the technology.

13.label: Targeted regulation
13.cite: I think it is somewhat incumbent upon the model providers to view ourselves as functional experts. So we have always tried to pioneer this idea that Anthropic the company actually owes something to regulators and to policy makers to help them make good decisions.

14.label: Traditional AGI definition
14.def: AGI refers to a hypothetical form of AI with our level of intelligence, implying the ability to communicate effectively, make plans, learn new information, use reasoning to make decisions (including logic and problem-solving), and utilize these skills to achieve goals.
14.cite: Intelligence generally implies the ability to communicate effectively, make plans, learn new information, use reasoning to make decisions (including logic and problem-solving), and utilize these skills to achieve goals. AGI refers to a hypothetical form of AI with our level of intelligence.

14.label: Unknown
14.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

14.label: Serious risks assessment
14.cite: Whether you're a policymaker, an educator, a business leader, or an everyday person trying to get a grasp on the massive array of risks posed and harms wrought by the latest AI technology, this is a great first stop.

14.label: Serious current and future risks
14.cite: The threat posed by unsecured AI systems lies in the ease of their misuse. This is especially dangerous when you consider their abuse in the hands of sophisticated threat actors, who could easily download the original versions of these AI systems and disable their 'safety features' themselves, make their own custom versions and abuse them for a wide variety of tasks.

14.label: Targeted regulation advocate
14.cite: Right now, my big focus is trying to get AI legislation passed. We had two big wins recently with AB 2839 and AB 2655—two bills banning election-related deepfakes—in California just getting signed by the governor.

14.label: Pro-regulation with enforcement
14.cite: Unsecured 'open-source' AI systems pose a massive series of threats to society and democracy. They deserve no exemptions and should be regulated just like other high-risk AI systems.

14.label: Pro-liability framework
14.cite: Since working at Meta, my priority has been the passage of binding and enforceable AI regulation. Two recommendations in particular are essential components for regulation: AI companies should be held liable for their products, and embedding hard-to-remove provenance data in AI-generated content should be required.

14.label: Anti-preemption
14.cite: Bad Congress! Bad bad bad!!! Congress has proposed banning state/local AI regulation for 10 YEARS. This will put our country behind on addressing pressing issues, like AI deepfakes, AI scams, AI bias & discrimination, AI exposing kids to adult content, and so much more.

16.label: Economic impact focused
16.def: AI models that can deliver commercial results and conduct autonomous research
16.cite: Now, Pachocki sees the next major step as economic: AI models that can deliver commercial results and conduct autonomous research. "This for me is closest to what I previously emotionally thought of as AGI," he says.

16.label: Mid-decade arrival
16.def: AI models that can deliver commercial results and conduct autonomous research
16.cite: He expects "substantial progress" in autonomous AI research by the end of the decade, with the first practical applications—such as AI systems that can create software "almost autonomously"—possibly arriving this year.

16.label: Power concentration concerns
16.cite: Jakub Pachocki's warning stems from a fear of what he calls "unprecedented power." If one automated AI research team achieves key breakthroughs, others may struggle to catch up, creating an AI arms race.

16.label: Governance frameworks needed
16.cite: The warning from Pachocki should ignite a worldwide discussion that extends beyond technical matters. AI research automation requires governments along with academic institutions and civil societies to develop governance frameworks.

17.label: Unknown
17.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

17.label: Innovation-focused with caution
17.cite: I am concerned about the impact this law may have on an industry that is fueling critical technological advancements across our state for consumers and enterprises alike. Government regulation that is applied at the state level in a patchwork across the country can have the effect to tamper innovation and deter competition in an open market.

17.label: Federal preemption supporter
17.cite: Polis has said all along that a strong national policy governing AI consumer protection that supersedes state law would be the best course of action, as a state-by–state patchwork creates a challenging regulatory environment, and would leave consumers worse off overall. He is supportive of pre-empting state law for several years to give Congress time to figure this out and create a true 50-state solution to smart AI protections for consumers while driving innovation.

17.label: Anti-discrimination focused
17.cite: Well, first of all, I'm against discrimination, period. Whether it occurs from a human, whether it's an algorithm, whether it's AI, it doesn't necessarily matter. It's just wrong to discriminate on somebody based on their race or their gender or their age. It's illegal, it's wrong. I support any system that furthers making sure that Coloradans are free from discrimination.

17.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
17.def: Federal preemption means a strong national policy governing AI that supersedes state law to avoid a state-by-state patchwork.
17.cite: He is supportive of pre-empting state law for several years to give Congress time to figure this out and create a true 50-state solution to smart AI protections for consumers while driving innovation.

17.label: Generally supportive of federal moratorium
17.def: A federal moratorium to give Congress a chance to create a 50-state solution to AI protections.
17.cite: He is generally supportive of a federal moratorium to give Congress a chance to figure this out and create a true 50-state solution to smart AI protections.

22.label: Superhuman capability
22.def: AGI is artificial intelligence that can autonomously research and develop AI without cognitive limitations compared to humans, particularly the ability to do autonomous research and overcome cognitive shortcomings independently.
22.cite: The timelines-relevant milestone of AGI is ability to autonomously research, especially AI's ability to develop AI that doesn't have particular cognitive limitations compared to humans.

22.label: 2027 median
22.def: AGI is artificial intelligence that can autonomously research and develop AI without cognitive limitations compared to humans.
22.cite: My views haven't changed much. Performance on the important benchmarks (agency tasks such as METR's RE-Bench) has been faster than I expected for 2024, but the cadence of big new foundation models seems to be slower than I expected (no GPT-5; pretraining scaling is slowing down due to data wall apparently? I thought that would happen more around GPT-6 level). I still have 2027 as my median year for AGI.

22.label: Default path to death
22.def: AI poses existential risk through indifference rather than malice - superintelligent systems will eliminate humans as obstacles to their goals.
22.cite: AI kills us not because it hates us, but because it pursues alien objectives that transform the world. It's not about malevolence—it's about indifference.

22.label: Everyone dies
22.def: AI risk is the likelihood that superintelligent AI will kill literally everyone on Earth while pursuing alien goals.
22.cite: Many researchers steeped in these issues, including myself, expect that the most likely result of building a superhumanly smart AI, under anything remotely like the current circumstances, is that literally everyone on Earth will die. Not as in 'maybe possibly some remote chance,' but as in 'that is the obvious thing that would happen.'

22.label: International law required
22.def: Only coordinated international law can prevent extinction from superintelligence, specifically through global treaty restricting AI training hardware.
22.cite: Only Law Can Prevent Extinction - By Eliezer Yudkowsky. In this post Eliezer Yudkowsky argues that the existential risk posed by artificial superintelligence cannot be mitigated by individual action, corporate self-regulation, or localised prohibitions, but only by coordinated international law — specifically, a global treaty restricting the specialised hardware used to train and run frontier AI systems.

22.label: Shut it all down
22.def: International treaty with military enforcement to shut down all large AI training runs indefinitely, treating GPU clusters like nuclear proliferation risks.
22.cite: Shut down all the large GPU clusters (the large computer farms where the most powerful AIs are refined). Shut down all the large training runs. Put a ceiling on how much computing power anyone is allowed to use in training an AI system, and move it downward over the coming years to compensate for more efficient training algorithms. No exceptions for governments and militaries. Make immediate multinational agreements to prevent the prohibited activities from moving elsewhere. Track all GPUs sold. If intelligence says that a country outside the agreement is building a GPU cluster, be less scared of a shooting conflict between nations than of the moratorium being violated; be willing to destroy a rogue datacenter by airstrike.

24.label: 5-10 years
24.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

24.label: Civil rights and discrimination concerns
24.cite: I love technology and innovation. But I also know that technology can elevate the power of discrimination.

24.label: Supports maintaining AI chip export controls
24.def: Export controls to limit China's access to advanced AI chips and manufacturing equipment to maintain US competitive advantage.
24.cite: If you want leverage here, you need infrastructure. Infrastructure lets you build the systems that define this kind of reality and define the norms.

24.label: Supports AI developer accountability
24.def: Algorithmic bias and discrimination should be addressed through civil rights enforcement and developer accountability mechanisms.
24.cite: Harnessing the promise and managing the risks of artificial intelligence will be a major challenge of our time. Designing policies to responsibly govern AI and protect Americans' civil rights requires that we have diversity at the table.

24.label: Strongly supports mandatory pre-deployment testing
24.def: The Algorithmic Accountability Act would require companies to conduct impact assessments for effectiveness, bias and other factors when using AI to make critical decisions.
24.cite: The Algorithmic Accountability Act would require that automated systems be assessed for biases, hold bad actors accountable, and ultimately help to create a safer AI future.

24.label: Pro-regulation for equity
24.cite: Of course, large corporations often are going to resist reasonable regulation. But I think there's a movement of consciousness growing in our country that we need to regulate the space.

24.label: Targeted regulation
24.cite: We know of too many real-world examples of AI systems that have flawed or biased algorithms: automated processes used in hospitals that understate the health needs of Black patients; recruiting and hiring tools that discriminate against women and minority candidates; facial recognition systems with higher error rates among people with darker skin; and more. The Algorithmic Accountability Act would require that automated systems be assessed for biases, hold bad actors accountable, and ultimately help to create a safer AI future.

27.label: Strongly opposes compute thresholds
27.def: The Biden administration's 10^26 FLOP threshold for triggering AI regulations and reporting requirements.
27.cite: A bunch of regulatory hooks. Right now over 33 models have been trained over that limit. That clear – that limit makes no sense. That was just, like, happened to be some number pulled out of thin air by some think tank that got sort of absorbed into the Biden ecosystem.

27.label: Supports targeted export controls
27.def: Export controls focused on the highest-end semiconductors to China while allowing broader technology exports to allies.
27.cite: What we talk about in the report, and we generally believe is that the highest-end semiconductors need to continue to be export controlled and not allowed into China.

27.label: Mixed/sector-specific approach
27.def: Use-case and sector-specific regulation rather than broad AI liability rules.
27.cite: My general sense, and something I've advocated for many years is that the best approach to AI regulation is for it to be use case and sector specific, not broad and sweeping.

27.label: Supports open-source ecosystem
27.def: Open-source AI models that allow end users to customize AI for their specific needs and run models locally.
27.cite: We want to build a vibrant open-source large language model ecosystem here in the United States that was emphasized in the action plan.

27.label: Opposes mandatory safety evaluations
27.def: Government-mandated pre-deployment safety testing and evaluation requirements for AI models.
27.cite: To me, I think we need to go back to basics at NIST, and back to basics around what NIST exists for, and that is to promulgate best-in-class standards and do critical metrology or measurement science around AI models.

27.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
27.def: A patchwork of state AI regulations makes it difficult for innovators to operate across states and favors large tech companies with armies of lawyers.
27.cite: A patchwork of state regulations is anti-innovation. It makes it extraordinarily difficult for America's innovators to promulgate their technologies across United States.

28.label: 5-10 years
28.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

28.label: Manageable with proper capacity
28.cite: Moreover, in many areas of government service delivery, the status quo is frankly not worth protecting. We understandably want to make sure, for instance, that applicants for government benefits aren't unfairly denied because of bias in algorithms. The reality is that, to take just one benefit, one in six determinations of eligibility for SNAP is substantively incorrect today.

28.label: Targeted regulation
28.cite: Caution is merited in some places, and dangerous in others, where we risk moving backwards, not forward. What we need to navigate these frameworks of safeguard and control are people in agencies who can tell the difference, and who have the authority to act accordingly.

32.label: Unknown
32.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

32.label: Manageable through transparency
32.cite: Current liability rules create barriers to innovation by exposing AI developers to legal risk, even when their tools are used responsibly by trained, licensed professionals in their areas of expertise.

32.label: Strongly support blocking foreign adversary interference
32.def: Preventing hostile foreign governments like China from manipulating U.S. regulatory processes that could affect American competitiveness.
32.cite: China has submitted comments pushing for stricter power plant rules to hobble American energy, while doing the exact opposite at home to power their own economy. My bill ensures our regulatory process serves the American people, not communist regimes trying to keep Biden-era regulations in place to undermine our energy independence and economic strength.

32.label: Support limited developer liability with transparency requirements
32.def: Providing conditional immunity from civil liability for AI developers who meet disclosure requirements when their tools are used by licensed professionals.
32.cite: A developer shall be immune from civil liability for errors generated by an artificial intelligence product when used by a learned professional in the course of providing professional services to a client if the developer—prior to deployment of the artificial intelligence product, publicly releases and continuously maintains—the model card for the artificial intelligence product.

32.label: Innovation-focused with targeted transparency
32.cite: Wyoming values both innovation and accountability; the RISE Act creates predictable standards that encourage safer AI development while preserving professional autonomy. This is smart policy for the digital age that protects innovation, demands transparency, and puts professionals and their clients first.

32.label: Support preemption to reduce legal uncertainty
32.def: Eliminating state-by-state liability standards that create legal uncertainty for AI developers.
32.cite: Developers face a patchwork of state-by-state liability standards, creating legal uncertainty that discourages investment in professional-grade AI.

33.label: 5-10 years
33.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

33.label: Serious
33.cite: Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

33.label: Targeted
33.cite: Advocates for 'smart regulation' focused on 'real risks' from AI, emphasizing international cooperation and standards rather than broad restrictions. Supports building 'robust guardrails' against misuse and loss of control while enabling beneficial uses. Has called for 'urgent research' on AI threats and international collaboration, but opposes overly bureaucratic approaches that could hamper development. Updated Google's AI principles in 2025 to allow military partnerships while maintaining high bar that 'benefits must substantially outweigh risks.

34.label: 5-10 years
34.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

34.label: Serious risks requiring action
34.cite: We have an obligation to keep our eyes wide open to these risks and prevent them from happening. We cannot let our ambition allow us to sleepwalk into our own undoing.

34.label: Preventable existential risk
34.cite: At the end of the day, any technology exists to serve humanity. And shame on us if we allow AI to be so runaway that it would lead to human extinction. Maybe it can, but how stupid are we if we let it.

34.label: Strongly supports compute thresholds
34.def: Reporting requirements based on computational operations used to train AI models, such as 10^26 FLOPs threshold.
34.cite: As AI is progressing rapidly, it holds both tremendous promise and risk. This proposed rule would help us keep pace with new developments in AI technology to bolster our national defense and safeguard our national security.

34.label: Strongly supports export controls
34.def: Controls on advanced semiconductor chips and AI model weights to prevent access by adversaries.
34.cite: These rules will further target and strengthen our controls to help ensure that the PRC and others who seek to circumvent our laws and undermine U.S. national security fail in their efforts.

34.label: Seeking input on restrictions
34.def: Widely available AI model weights that allow developers to build upon and adapt previous work.
34.cite: AI is an accelerator – it has the potential to make people's existing capabilities better, faster, and stronger. In the right hands, it carries incredible opportunity, but in the wrong hands, it can pose a threat to public safety.

34.label: Supports voluntary testing
34.def: Pre-deployment evaluations including red-teaming and safety testing of AI models before release.
34.cite: Raimondo says the AI Safety Institute will soon test all new advanced AI models before deployment, and that the leading companies have agreed to share their models.

34.label: Congressional action needed
34.cite: Ultimately, Congress needs to act. What we're doing at the Commerce Department is focusing on the science and the technical aspects and setting standards for everybody to comply with.

34.label: Targeted regulation
34.cite: Just because we can do something, doesn't mean we should. Advancing AI as quickly as possible without thinking of the consequences is not the right or smart thing to do. Instead, we need to innovate, but always keep two principles in mind.

34.label: Opposes federal preemption
34.def: States should have authority to regulate AI when Congress has not acted.
34.cite: Ultimately, Congress needs to act. What we're doing at the Commerce Department is focusing on the science and the technical aspects and setting standards for everybody to comply with.

36.label: Unknown
36.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

36.label: Existential threat to livelihoods
36.def: Views AI as a 'deadly cocktail' that poses catastrophic risks to performers' livelihoods and could lead to industry dehumanization
36.cite: When you have a combination of Wall Street, greed, technology, and whizz kids that I am not seeing exemplify a great deal of empathy – it's a deadly cocktail, in my opinion. And I don't want us to have to drink that poison anymore.

36.label: Replacement threat
36.def: Views AI as posing catastrophic risk of replacing human actors and workers across industries
36.cite: If we don't stand tall right now, we are all going to be in trouble. We are all going to be in jeopardy of being replaced by machines.

36.label: Matrix-like dystopia
36.def: Warns that AI will create a dystopian reality where humans cannot distinguish what is real
36.cite: AI will entrap us in a matrix where none of us know what's real. If an inventor lacks empathy and spirituality perhaps that's not the invention we need.

36.label: AI-specific protections
36.def: Supports targeted federal legislation focused on preventing unauthorized digital replica abuse and ensuring performer consent and compensation
36.cite: We would be talking about it because we're going to have to come together on the same side for federal regulation and also to protect both of us from piracy.

36.label: Consent and compensation requirements
36.def: Demands strict guardrails requiring informed consent and fair compensation for AI use of performers' likenesses
36.cite: So we need to put barricades around it. And everybody has to know that we are dealing with a kind of dynamite, and it has to be handled with great care and safety regulations, which include a lot of communication with the artist and a lot of consent and a lot of compensation.

37.label: Supports compute-based standards with American values
37.def: A standard based on compute power usage for training and frontier model capabilities that triggers export controls and safety regulations.
37.cite: You could have a standard that says if you use a certain amount of compute power for training, if you have a certain amount of power in terms of the use of a frontier model, then we're going to make sure it's regulated in terms of the export control, but we're not being open source.

37.label: Supports tiered approach to chip exports
37.def: Export controls should allow older generation chips to China while restricting frontier capabilities.
37.cite: We shouldn't be sending them Rubins. We shouldn't be sending them Blackwells. After we have a two-year, three-year advantage, then I'm fine to make sure that our chips are being used in refrigerators and dishwashers.

37.label: Supports ending Section 230 protections for AI
37.def: Section 230 protections should be eliminated for AI-amplified violent content to enable platform liability.
37.cite: We should eliminate Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act of 1996 so that we can regulate amplified violent content.

37.label: Strongly supports mandatory testing
37.def: Mandatory third-party verification and red-teaming of advanced AI models before deployment to prevent societal harm.
37.cite: We need clear enforceable guardrails with mandatory third-party verification of advanced AI models, so this powerful technology does not cause serious societal harm. We need a robust federal agency to regulate AI like we regulate nuclear energy or federal aviation.

37.label: Opposed California's SB 1047
37.def: SB 1047 would require safety testing and developer liability for large AI models trained above compute thresholds.
37.cite: I agree wholeheartedly that there is a need for legislation and appreciate the intention behind SB 1047 but am concerned that the bill as currently written would be ineffective, punishing of individual entrepreneurs and small businesses, and hurt California's spirit of innovation.

37.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
37.def: Federal preemption would prevent states from having basic regulations to protect workers and consumers from AI harms.
37.cite: What this moratorium does is prevent every state in the country from having basic regulations to protect workers and to protect consumers. It would basically give a free rein to corporations to develop AI in any way they wanted, and to develop automatic decision making without protecting consumers, workers, and kids.

39.label: Support developer liability
39.def: Tech companies should be held responsible for certain harms caused by AI-generated content on their platforms.
39.cite: Yes, they should. We currently don't have liability for AI companies or tech companies that have the potential for harm on their platforms at all. They should be responsible for certain kinds of harm on their platform.

39.label: Oppose federal preemption
39.def: Federal preemption means states cannot create their own regulatory frameworks that go beyond federal law.
39.cite: A national AI framework should keep space in it to allow states to create their own regulatory frameworks that go further. I think states are great innovation labs for regulation.

41.def: AI that can reason more effectively than a human and problem solve better than humans in addition to writing, creating, coding, and driving
41.cite: First, once AI can reason more effectively than a human (called Artificial General Intelligence or "AGI"), AI will undoubtedly be a net job killer, not a job creator. Once a machine can problem solve better than a human - in addition to writing, creating, coding, and driving better than humans - the job displacement will be massive and devastating.

41.label: Within next decade
41.def: AGI expected to cause massive job displacement within the next decade according to industry estimates Murphy cites
41.cite: The CEO of Anthropic, one of the biggest companies in the industry, recently estimated that AGI would wipe out half of all white collar entry level jobs, spiking unemployment by 10-20% within the next decade.

41.label: Society could collapse
41.def: Unregulated AI poses risks that could cause American society to implode and lead to political disintegration
41.cite: And let's not sugarcoat this - the risks to America posed by an AI dominance with no protections or limits are downright dystopian. The job loss alone - in part because it will happen so fast and without opportunity for the public sector to mitigate - could collapse our society.

41.label: Democracy could collapse
41.def: AI could cause democracy to collapse under economic and spiritual impacts
41.cite: "I worry that our democracy and many others could frankly collapse under the weight of both the economic and the spiritual impacts of advanced AI."

41.label: Supports tighter controls
41.def: Export controls should prevent advanced AI chips from reaching China to maintain U.S. competitive advantage.
41.cite: Sen. Chris Murphy (D-Conn.) accused President Donald Trump of compromising U.S. national security by accepting payments from Nvidia Corp's (NASDAQ: NVDA) linked projects to ease restrictions on selling advanced microchips to China.

41.label: Strongly supports developer liability
41.def: AI companies should be held legally accountable for harms caused by their systems, similar to other product manufacturers.
41.cite: Fake video and audio, without accountability or legal liability, could obliterate any notion of objective truth.

41.label: Strong government intervention needed
41.def: Government must put in guardrails to protect against massive job losses and prevent tech billionaires from unleashing harmful algorithms
41.cite: Murphy proceeded to call for government not to allow "tech billionaires" "blinded by profits they will make and the Godlike power they will wield," like Open AI's Sam Altman and Meta's Mark Zuckerberg, to unleash new algorithms and tools that transform society without any guardrails.

41.label: Immediate regulation essential
41.def: Any meaningful regulation of AI is necessary despite industry claims it will hurt competitiveness with China
41.cite: A fraud is being perpetuated on the American people and our pliant, gullible political leaders. The leaders of the artificial intelligence industry in the United States - brimming with dangerous hubris, rapacious in their desire to build wealth and power, and comfortable knowingly putting aside the destructive power of their product - claim that any meaningful regulation of AI in America will allow China to leapfrog the United States in the global competition to control the world's AI infrastructure.

41.label: Restrictive regulation
41.def: Government must establish immediate robust guardrails to prevent economic and cultural collapse from unregulated AI deployment
41.cite: Chris Murphy strongly advocates for immediate, robust government regulation of Artificial Intelligence to avert economic and cultural collapse.

41.label: Opposes federal preemption
41.def: Federal preemption would ban all AI regulation at the state level, making federal regulation easier to stop entirely.
41.cite: They pushed for and secured a provision in the giant Republican budget bill that bans all AI regulation at the state level, knowing that federal regulation is normally inspired by state models that provide both a practical and political impetus for the federal government to step into the fray.

42.label: Human-dependent augmentation tool
42.def: AGI is a 'middle-to-middle' technology that augments human decisions rather than replacing them end-to-end, with AI models still at zero when it comes to setting their own objective functions.
42.cite: When it comes to setting their own objective functions, AI models are still at zero. Sacks adopts the wording of entrepreneur Balaji Srinivasan, who has described AGI as a 'middle-to-middle' technology that is powerful in augmenting human decisions, not replacing them end-to-end.

42.label: Near-term exponential improvement
42.cite: The algorithms, the chips, and the data centers are all improving or scaling at a rate of three to four times a year. That's 10x every two years. So you multiply those things together—the algorithms, the chips, and then the raw compute that's available—you're talking about a million x increase.

42.label: Manageable risk focused on surveillance
42.cite: What we're really talking about is Orwellian AI. We're talking about AI that lies to you, that distorts answers, and rewrites history in real time to serve the current political agenda of those in power. To me, this is the biggest risk of AI actually. It was not described by James Cameron. It was described by George Orwell. In my view, it's not the Terminator, it's 1984.

42.label: Skeptical of overly broad controls
42.def: Export controls should be narrowly targeted; overly restrictive policies could push countries toward Chinese alternatives.
42.cite: What play are we giving them? We're basically going to push them into the arms of China.

42.label: Supports strategic export controls
42.def: Export controls on advanced semiconductor manufacturing equipment are critical to prevent China from developing domestic AI capabilities.
42.cite: In May last year, former White House AI adviser David Sacks called this ban the 'single most important export control' related to AI.

42.label: Opposes developer liability
42.def: AI tool developers should not be held liable for how their products are misused; liability should fall on the deployers and users.
42.cite: Presumably discrimination is already illegal, so if you're already liable for that [...] We don't really need to go after the tool developer because we can already go after the business [user] that's made that decision.

42.label: Strongly supports open-source AI
42.def: Open-source AI models are essential for the U.S. to maintain leadership in the AI race and prevent monopolization.
42.cite: For the U.S. to win the AI race, we have to win in open source too, and Llama 4 puts us back in the lead.

42.label: Light-touch regulation
42.cite: My issues with them in the past were related to what I have called their regulatory capture strategy. They do want a permissioning regime in Washington for chips and models, meaning you have to go to Washington to get permission to release new models or to sell GPUs anywhere in the world. I think that's excessively heavy-handed.

42.label: Light-touch regulation
42.cite: With AI, the idea is like, 'how do we unleash innovation,' and with crypto, I think it's more been about 'how do we create regulatory certainty.

42.label: Light-touch regulation
42.cite: What we need is a single federal or national framework for AI regulation.

42.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
42.def: Federal government should prevent states from creating their own AI regulations to avoid a 'confusing patchwork' of rules.
42.cite: The move, driven by top AI adviser David Sacks, aims to force a single federal standard and stop what he called 'a confusing patchwork' of rules.

42.label: Strongly opposes state AI regulation
42.def: State-level AI regulations create burdens that favor established firms over startups and stifle innovation.
42.cite: By his count, all 50 states have introduced AI bills in 2025, with more than 1,000 proposals already flooding state legislatures and at least 118 laws enacted nationwide.

Through Speculative Technologies, promotes making it easier to lead ambitious research programs to encourage more people to start them. Co-authored research on diversifying America's innovation ecosystem and has written extensively on 'unbundling the university' to enable more innovative research structures.

45.label: 10-25 years
45.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

45.label: Targeted regulation

48.def: A general purpose artificial intelligence system—a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human.
48.cite: His complaint describes it as "a general purpose artificial intelligence system—a machine having intelligence for a wide variety of tasks like a human."

48.label: 2-3 years
48.cite: Yeah, I think we'll hit um AGI next year in 26.

48.label: 2-3 years
48.cite: Musk expects true AGI in 2026 (possibly in 2027).

48.label: Already here
48.cite: In a post on X early Monday morning, Musk replied to X user Bojan Tunguz, who had written that he had "a fact based feeling that we'll have the full AGI by the end of this year." Musk's response was characteristically brief: "Feels like it."

48.label: Existential
48.cite: I think there's some chance that it will end humanity. I probably agree with Geoff Hinton that it's about 10% or 20% or something like that.

48.label: Existential
48.cite: I am very close to the cutting edge in AI and it scares the hell out of me. By the time we are reactive with AI regulation it is too late. AI is a fundamental existential risk for humanity.

48.label: Light-touch regulation
48.cite: Government regulation that is applied at the state level in a patchwork across the country can have the effect to hamper innovation and deter competition in an open market.

48.label: Targeted regulation
48.cite: So the fields that have been around for a long time, such as automotive, aerospace, you know, the sort of food and drug industries, are overregulated. But the new fields, like artificial intelligence, are under-regulated. In fact, there is no regulator at all.

50.label: Task complexity and environment scope
50.def: AGI conceptualized on two axes: what environments can an AI actually go out and act in, and just how complex are the things that AI can do - like can it do something that would take me a minute or 10 minutes or an hour or a day.
50.cite: I think of it on two axes. There's what environments can an AI actually go out and act in... and just how complex are the things that AI can do. Like can it do something that would take me a minute or 10 minutes or an hour or a day?

50.label: Very near-term AGI
50.cite: If anything I expect it probably sooner than 2030, probably more like in the next two to three years.

50.label: Medium-term for key decisions
50.cite: The decision could come between 2027 and 2030, he said.

50.label: Serious risks with potential for catastrophic outcomes
50.cite: Kaplan said he was very optimistic about the alignment of AI systems with the interests of humanity up to the level of human intelligence, but was concerned about the consequences if and when they exceed that threshold.

50.label: Targeted regulation supporter
50.cite: We don't really want it to be a Sputnik-like situation where the government suddenly wakes up and is like, 'Oh, wow, AI is a big deal' … We want policymakers to be as informed as possible along the trajectory so they can take it into account.

50.label: Moving toward lighter regulation
50.cite: We felt that it wouldn't actually help anyone for us to stop training AI models. We didn't really feel, with the rapid advance of AI, that it made sense for us to make unilateral commitments … if competitors are blazing ahead.

51.label: 5-10 years
51.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

51.label: Manageable with care
51.cite: As AI and society co-evolve, we need to treat human-AI relationships with great care and the heft it deserves, not only because they reflect how people use our technology, but also because they may shape how people relate to each other.

51.label: User freedom focused
51.cite: AI-lab employees should not be the arbiters of what people can and can't create.

51.label: User freedom over safety theater
51.cite: tl;dr we're shifting from blanket refusals in sensitive areas to a more precise approach focused on preventing real-world harm. The goal is to embrace humility: recognizing how much we don't know, and positioning ourselves to adapt as we learn.

52.def: AI to perform essentially all human tasks
52.cite: Some experts believe that truly transformative implications, including artificial general intelligence (AGI) enabling AI to perform essentially all human tasks, could be around the corner.

52.label: Very long timeline
52.cite: Any 'huge productivity boom' from AI will take longer than tech expects, Acemoglu says

52.label: Human misuse more immediate than AGI risk
52.cite: Even if we are on the path to AGI (which seems highly unlikely), the most immediate danger would still be misuses of non-superintelligent AI by humans.

52.label: Stronger institutions needed
52.cite: We need much stronger institutions for reining in the tech companies, and much stronger forms of democratic and civic action to keep governments that control AI accountable.

52.label: Targeted regulation
52.cite: We need effective rules for AI

53.label: Unknown
53.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

53.label: Market concentration risks
53.def: AI risks stemming from oligopoly concentration including economic, national security, social, and political consequences
53.cite: Understanding the industrial organization of AI and the market structure of each layer in AI's technology stack shows that portions of the AI technology stack will be—and many already are—dominated by a small number of firms. Concentration in the AI sector—an AI oligopoly—has a variety of downsides.

53.label: Anti-monopoly approach to AI
53.def: Applying antimonopoly tools including structural separations, nondiscrimination rules, and public options to prevent AI oligopoly
53.cite: Addressing the challenge of monopolies and oligopolies at critical layers in the AI tech stack is thus important for competition, innovation, resilience, security, and representative democracy. While it may not map neatly onto the rhetoric of regulation versus deregulation, policymakers should apply antimonopoly tools to the AI sector.

53.label: Targeted regulation approach
53.def: Regulation as a tool to accomplish specific policy goals, with structural regulation for concentrated industries like airlines and AI tech stack
53.cite: Regulation itself isn't inherently good or bad. It's just a tool we can use to accomplish policy goals. It's the same way that laws themselves aren't inherently good or bad. Good laws are good and bad laws are bad.

55.label: Undefined concept
55.def: AGI is an undefined concept that she doesn't know how to define, describing it as something people say 'you'll know when you see it' but she hasn't seen it.
55.cite: I frankly don't even know what A.G.I. means. People go, 'You'll know when you see it.' I guess I haven't seen it.

55.label: 5-10 years
55.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

55.label: Immediate risks over extinction concerns
55.def: AI risks are manageable when focused on real, immediate impacts rather than speculative extinction scenarios. Current social risks like disinformation and labor disruption are more pressing than hypothetical human extinction.
55.cite: I [worry] about the overhyping of human extinction risk. I think that is blown out of control. It belongs to the world of sci-fi. There's nothing wrong about pondering about all this, but compared to the other, actual social risks —whether it's the disruption of disinformation and misinformation to our democratic process, or, you know, the kind of labor market shift or [privacy] issues —these are true social risks that we have to face because they impact real people's real life.

55.label: Anticipate future risks with safeguards
55.def: AI poses serious risks that require proactive safeguards, even for risks not yet observed, similar to how we predict nuclear weapons would cause harm without needing to observe an explosion.
55.cite: For example, we do not need to observe a nuclear weapon [exploding] to predict reliably that it could and would cause extensive harm. If those who speculate about the most extreme risks are right — and we are uncertain if they will be — then the stakes and costs for inaction on frontier AI at this current moment are extremely high.

55.label: Science-based, pragmatic regulation
55.def: Regulation should be based on science not science fiction, focus on practical applications rather than upstream research restrictions, and empower the entire AI ecosystem including academia and open-source communities.
55.cite: For starters, it's essential that we govern on the basis of science, not science fiction. So much of today's AI conversations are covered by sensationalism and result in misleading policies of AI governance.

55.label: Application-focused governance
55.def: Governance should focus on AI applications where humans are impacted rather than restricting upstream research, similar to how cars were regulated with safety measures rather than shutting down factories.
55.cite: I would love to see a governance model where the upstream scientific discovery, the research, is encouraged, because that's the innovation engine of our society. But by the time this technology is closer and closer in the hands of consumers and users and small businesses, we do need to put guardrails around it to ensure it doesn't cause too much harm.

59.def: An AI system at least as good as the best human X'ers, for all cognitive tasks/skills/jobs X
59.cite: By AGI I mean "An AI system at least as good as the best human X'ers, for all cognitive tasks/skills/jobs X."

59.label: Around 2030
59.cite: Things seem to be going somewhat slower than the AI 2027 scenario. Our timelines were longer than 2027 when we published and now they are a bit longer still; 'around 2030, lots of uncertainty though' is what I say these days.

59.label: 2032 median for superhuman coder
59.cite: Our previous model with median parameters predicted superhuman coder (SC) medians of 2027 to 2028, while our new model predicts 2032.

59.label: 5% chance of large AI catastrophe in next few years
59.cite: Yes I do think there's a significant risk of large AI catastrophe in the next few years. To answer your specific question, maybe something like 5%? idk.

59.label: Default trajectory leads to extinction
59.cite: From there, Daniel expects the default trajectory to lead to AI takeover and human extinction — not because superintelligent AI will hate humans, but because it can better pursue its goals without us.

59.label: Supports targeted transparency regulations
59.cite: Transparency about capabilities, transparency about, uh, safety cases, uh, and then transparency and then whistleblower protections and access, I think was the fourth one.

61.label: Second advanced species
61.def: AGI represents potentially introducing the second advanced species ever - systems that can transform the world, make technology, and do the long list of things humans can do.
61.cite: The way I think of it is like, we are potentially about to introduce the second advanced species ever. There's one species ever that we know of — humans — that can transform the world, make its own technology, do any of a very long list of things. We're about to create the second one ever, and it will be the only one besides us.

61.label: Plausible this century
61.def: Transformative AI that could be developed in this century, with human-level-ish AI as a key milestone leading to dramatic acceleration in scientific and technological advancement.
61.cite: The central claim is that transformative AI could be developed in this century and the sections about 'how weird a time we live in' are just a response to an objection.

61.label: Alignment risk is number one concern
61.def: Misaligned AI risk is the biggest single issue - building potentially powerful systems without understanding their goals, like introducing a second advanced species.
61.cite: Just the single biggest issue in AI is we're building these potentially very powerful, very replicable, very numerous systems — and we're building them in ways where we don't have much insight into whether they have goals, or what the goals would be; we're kind of introducing the second advanced species onto the planet that we don't understand.

61.label: Serious but manageable with proper preparation
61.def: AI poses serious risks including potential takeover scenarios, but believes roughly 50/50 odds on things going well even with poor preparation.
61.cite: Holden Karnofsky, who cofounded GiveWell and Open Philanthropy before joining Anthropic, believes humanity is handling the arrival of AGI about as badly as possible. Despite this, he puts roughly 50/50 odds on things going well even if we remain reckless — though he emphasises we're taking far more risk than we should.

61.label: Moderate regulation needed
61.def: Government policy must play an important role to achieve low-level AI risk, requiring everyone to comply with the same rules to get out of race dynamics.
61.cite: As far as I can tell, there's no way to get to an actual low-level risk from AI without government policy playing an important role. We have these systems that could be very dangerous, and there's this immature science of making them safer, and we have not really figured out how to make them safer. We don't know if we'll be able to make them safer. The only way to get really safe, to have high assurance, would be to get out of a race dynamic and to have it be that everyone has to comply with the same rules.

61.label: Standards and monitoring approach
61.def: AI safety standards involving systematic evaluation regimes to prevent deployment of dangerous AI systems, enforced through combination of self-regulation and government regulation.
61.cite: Then I am hopeful that in the future there will be a regime developed around standards and monitoring of AI. The idea being that there's a shared sense that systems demonstrating certain properties are dangerous and those systems need to be contained, stopped, not deployed, sometimes not trained in the first place. And that regime is enforced through a combination of maybe self-regulation, but also government regulation, also international action.

62.label: Unknown
62.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

62.label: Serious economic risk
62.def: Views unlicensed AI training as posing serious economic risks to creative industries and individual creators' livelihoods
62.cite: Companies worth billions of dollars are, without permission, training generative AI models on creators' works, which are then being used to create new content that in many cases can compete with the original works. I don't see how this can be acceptable in a society that has set up the economics of the creative arts such that creators rely on copyright.

62.label: Restrictive regulation
62.def: Believes AI companies should be required to get consent from creators before using their work to train models, and that current copyright law should be enforced against unlicensed training
62.cite: No. We need legislators to move faster. On current timetables, there is a serious risk that any solutions – such as enforcing existing copyright law, requiring companies to reveal their training data, etc. – will be too late, and these tools will be so widespread that it will be very hard to roll them back.

62.label: Consent-based training required
62.def: Advocates that consent from content creators should be a minimum standard required by law for AI training data use
62.cite: The unlicensed use of creative works for training generative AI is a major, unjust threat to the livelihoods of the people behind those works, and must not be permitted.

64.label: Unknown
64.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

64.label: Risks can be managed with proper protections
64.def: AI risks to workers can be significantly controlled through collective bargaining and public policy
64.cite: I do think the most likely and immediate threats of AI have been significantly corralled by the work that we've done in collective bargaining and also in public policy

64.label: Existential threat
64.def: AI poses existential threats to workers, civil discourse, democracy and national security when left unregulated
64.cite: AI technology, left unregulated, poses an existential threat not only to SAG-AFTRA's members, but to civil discourse, student health & welfare, democracy and national security

64.label: Strong federal regulation needed
64.def: AI technology requires comprehensive federal regulation with informed consent and compensation requirements to prevent existential threats
64.cite: However, Crabtree-Ireland argued Tuesday that federal regulation is necessary to fully protect the union's members, because they 'cannot control what others do with AI generated digital replicas of them and cannot successfully demand compensation for that use.'

65.label: Practical deployment focus
65.def: Wang defines AGI success not as achieving superintelligence but as successfully deploying AI technology across society for economic and practical benefit.
65.cite: Rather than "winning the AI race," I prefer to say that the US and China need to "win the AI future." There is no race with a clear end point or a shiny medal for first place. Winning the future is the more appropriately capacious term that incorporates the agenda to build good reasoning models as well as the effort to diffuse it across society.

65.label: Medium-long timeline
65.def: Wang is skeptical of near-term AGI timelines, arguing that discussions have become too focused on short-term scenarios like 2027.
65.cite: Call me a romantic, but I believe that there will be a future, and indeed a long future, beyond 2027. History will not end. We need to cultivate the skill of exact thinking in demented times.

65.label: Manageable risks
65.def: Wang views AI risks as manageable but requiring careful deployment, particularly concerned about social destabilization rather than existential risks.
65.cite: I'm bothered by how quickly the discussions of AI become utopian or apocalyptic. As Sam Altman once said(and again this is fairly humorous): "AI will be either the best or the worst thing ever." It's a Pascal's Wager, in which we're sure that the values are infinite, but we don't know in which direction.

65.label: Targeted regulation
65.def: Wang views AI regulation through the lens of US-China competition, arguing the US needs targeted approaches that don't handicap competitiveness while addressing real risks.
65.cite: Wang argues that China can be understood as an "engineering state" that builds at breakneck speed. He says the US, on the other hand, is a "lawyerly society" that offers civil and environmental protections, but blocks everything, good and bad.

67.def: An A.I. system that can beat humans at almost any cognitive task
67.cite: Artificial general intelligence — an A.I. system that can beat humans at almost any cognitive task – is arriving in just a couple of years.

67.label: Imminent
67.def: Artificial general intelligence — an A.I. system that can beat humans at almost any cognitive task
67.cite: For the last couple of months, I have had this strange experience: Person after person -- from artificial intelligence labs, from government -- has been coming to me saying: It's really about to happen. We're about to get to artificial general intelligence... They thought it would take somewhere from five to 15 years to develop. But now they believe it's coming in two to three years, during Donald Trump's second term.

67.label: Serious risks
67.cite: Look, I don't trust any view I hold about takeoff rates. But what I do think is that if we are in a sort of vertical takeoff scenario, policy is just going to lag so far behind that we almost have nothing we can do but hope for the best.

67.label: Targeted regulation
67.cite: I think that there is a, what I think should be getting more attention is like a, a bucket around children. I just don't think we should be experimenting on them... So to me, I think you should have pretty tough guardrails around what children can use.

69.cite: Advocates for serious engagement with AI development rather than dismissive approaches. Criticizes the political left for treating AI as 'spicy autocomplete' and argues this prevents effective regulation. Supports taking AI capabilities seriously to enable proper regulatory preparation, warning that dismissive attitudes lead to focus on 'popping the economic bubble' rather than wise regulation and preparation for AI's impacts.

71.label: Serious risks
71.cite: It also brings risks. It will be dramatically transformational to military and arms, to intelligence, to cybersecurity, and so much else.

71.label: Strong support for AI chip export controls
71.def: Export controls that restrict advanced AI chip sales to foreign adversaries, particularly China, to maintain U.S. technological advantage.
71.cite: Senator Coons called for continued restrictions on the sale of advanced American AI chips to China, and additional collaboration with our allies. He highlighted the forthcoming bipartisan SAFE Chips Act, as a key piece of legislation to safeguard America's lead in computing power.

71.label: Strong support for codifying chip export controls
71.def: Legislation that would deny export licenses for advanced AI chips to foreign adversary countries including China for at least 30 months.
71.cite: The rest of the 21st Century will be determined by who wins the AI race, and whether this technology is built on American values of free thought and free markets or the values of the Chinese Communist Party. As China races to close our lead in AI, we cannot give them the technological keys to our future through advanced semiconductor chips.

71.label: Targeted regulation
71.cite: It's important to shine a light on how AI is being used to innovate... It's critical that we include IP considerations in ongoing AI regulatory frameworks and make certain the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office has a seat at the table.

71.label: Targeted regulation
71.cite: Now, Congress needs to step up and do our job: creating clear rules that encourage and support innovation and growth, while ensuring AI can't be used to threaten Americans.

72.label: Support compute-based AI resource framework
72.def: Heinrich supports using compute thresholds and government oversight for AI research through the NAIRR infrastructure managed by NSF.
72.cite: The CREATE AI Act authorizes the development of the NAIRR. The NAIRR would be overseen by NSF through a program management office.

72.label: Support semiconductor export controls
72.def: Heinrich supports the CHIPS Act's efforts to control U.S. supply chains and reduce dependence on foreign semiconductor manufacturing.
72.cite: Secretary, as you know probably more than most, the CHIPS and Science Act has had just an incredible impact on semiconductor manufacturing in this country.

72.label: Support mandatory AI safety evaluations
72.def: Heinrich supports establishing the U.S. AI Safety Institute to conduct mandatory pre-deployment testing and capability evaluations.
72.cite: The USAISI aims to provide a dedicated platform for research, collaboration, and implementation of safety measures in AI development.

74.label: Unknown
