820.def: Deliberately avoids using the term AGI because it's often unhelpful and distracting, preferring to focus on increasingly capable AI systems rather than a singular moment
820.cite: I deliberately avoid using the term 'artificial general intelligence' (AGI), not because it's irrelevant, but because it's often unhelpful and distracting, even when used by leading researchers.

820.label: Already here
820.def: Views AGI as an unhelpful term, arguing that increasingly powerful AI systems are already arriving and the transition is already underway
820.cite: The evolution of AI systems is likely to accelerate exponentially over the next 12 months, rendering any discussion of a singular 'AGI' moment moot. The transition from narrow AI to increasingly capable systems will be measured in months, weeks, or even days.

820.label: Overstated
820.def: Views AI doom narrative as profoundly doubtful and largely unhelpful, questioning the extraordinary claims of existential risk
820.cite: As readers of Exponential View will know, I doubt it. In fact, I profoundly doubt it and largely believe the AI doom narrative is quite unhelpful.

820.label: Light-touch regulation
820.def: Favors innovation over precautionary regulation, arguing that excess precaution in AI regulation can cause more harm than it prevents
820.cite: Like nuclear energy, AI may carry risks, but also holds the potential to dramatically reduce others - and the greater harm may lie in not deploying AI applications rapidly and widely.

821.def: AI that is at least as good as humans at nearly all of the cognitive things that humans do
821.cite: I don't think there is agreement on what the term means. I use it to mean AI that is at least as good as humans at nearly all of the cognitive things that humans do.

821.label: Could be 5 years
821.cite: I used to think it was going to be like 20 to 50 years before we have general-purpose AI. Now I think it may be 20 years or less. It might even be 5 years.

821.label: 5 to 20 years, possibly 5
821.def: AI that is smarter than humans
821.cite: AI godfather Geoffrey Hinton believes that we might see AI that's smarter than humans in as little as five years. In a post on X last year, he wrote, 'I now predict 5 to 20 years but without much confidence. We live in very uncertain times.'

821.label: Real existential risk
821.cite: I think it's important that people understand it's not just science fiction; it's not just fear-mongering — it is a real risk that we need to think about, and we need to figure out in advance how to deal with it.

821.label: Over 50% existential risk
821.cite: In a Q&A session with METR (a research non-profit that focuses on assessing AI risks), Hinton stated that he thinks the risk of 'the existential threat' from AI is greater than 50%.

821.label: 10-20% chance AI takes control
821.cite: The AI pioneer estimates a 10% to 20% risk that artificial intelligence will eventually take control from humans.

821.label: Companies lobby against regulation
821.cite: If you look at what the big companies are doing right now, they're lobbying to get less AI regulation. There's hardly any regulation as it is, but they want less.

821.label: Strong global regulation needed
821.cite: Some argue that regulation acts as a brake, while unregulated AI accelerates progress. However, Hinton warned that this mindset is dangerous.

822.label: Human-level automated research
822.def: AGI as systems capable of automated AI/ML research that can match or exceed human researchers
822.cite: Our overall goal is to get to a point where we can automate alignment research. And what this doesn't mean is we're not trying to train a system that's really good at ML research, or that is really smart or something.

822.label: Medium-term AGI
822.def: AGI as systems that could arrive 'this decade' and become capable of automated ML research within a few years
822.cite: But a lot of us expect that actually things could be moving quite quickly, and systems could get a lot smarter or a lot more capable over the next few years.

822.label: Catastrophic risk

826.label: Serious risk requiring action
826.def: Matheny views AI as posing grave security challenges including cyber weapons, disinformation attacks, and biological weapons development, but frames these as manageable through proper oversight rather than existential threats.
826.cite: Applications of AI also pose grave security challenges for which we are currently unprepared, including the development of novel cyber weapons, large-scale disinformation attacks, and the design of advanced biological weapons.

826.label: Potentially dangerous but manageable
826.def: Matheny acknowledges AI as potentially dangerous with risks of catastrophic misuse, but emphasizes that risks can be substantially reduced through effective oversight and safety measures.
826.cite: While AI will bring many benefits, it is also potentially dangerous; it could be used to create cyber or bio weapons or to launch massive disinformation attacks. But the risks can be substantially reduced with effective oversight of three parts of the AI supply chain: hardware, training and deployment.

826.label: Targeted controls on AI
826.def: Matheny advocates for specific AI supply chain oversight focusing on hardware licensing, training assessment, and deployment review rather than broad restrictions.
826.cite: We would do well to establish a model of oversight for the world that focuses on the three parts of the AI supply chain. Increasing the safety of the American AI industry would boost public confidence at a time when consumers are growing wary of just what sort of future AI might bring.

826.label: Nonproliferation approach
826.def: Matheny frames AI regulation in terms of nonproliferation guardrails similar to nuclear technology controls, with targeted restrictions on computing hardware while allowing monitored cloud computing access.
826.cite: Like nuclear technologies, computing is a powerful dual-use technology that requires guardrails. Chip-export controls and manufacturing equipment-export controls can be seen as part of a nonproliferation guardrail for computing and AI.

831.label: AGI lacks definition

831.cite: AGI is a term that famously lacks a precise meaning, and certainly does not refer to any particular imminent technology.

831.label: Risk overstated

831.label: Focus on real harms

835.def: AI that is so smart that if a person can do some task, then AI can do it too - the point at which any intellectual task humans can perform, AI can also perform.
835.cite: It's the point at which AI is so smart that if a person can do some task, then AI can do it too. At that point you can say you have AGI.

835.def: A superintelligent learner that starts as a powerful learning system and becomes more capable through experience, like a 'superintelligent 15-year-old' rather than an omniscient oracle.
835.cite: Instead of imagining an all-knowing entity, think of AGI as: A superintelligent fifteen year old that can learn any job extremely fast. Not omniscient. Not preloaded with every skill. A learner that becomes smarter and more capable with experience.

835.label: Moderate regulation

Because he believes AI is going to become extremely powerful and transformative, Ilya Sutskever, co-founder and chief scientist of OpenAI, says the world will want to establish government regulations.

838.label: Focused on forecasting capabilities
838.def: Not explicitly defined, but refers to models gaining new capabilities through scaling and technical breakthroughs.
838.cite: One important question is forecasting the potential future capabilities of models. If you scale models up, they start to gain new capabilities that you didn't necessarily explicitly train for. You might have more technical breakthroughs, like the Strawberry model from OpenAI. This makes it difficult to forecast the pace of progress, and that matters a lot if we're trying to prepare for changes caused by AI systems.

His AI2050 project proposes the use of LLMs to investigate and explain the behavior of frontier models, detecting potential failure modes to prevent unintended consequences before advanced models are deployed in the real world.

838.label: Moderate

839.label: About 5 years

839.label: Stop AI development

842.def: AI that creates machines that are more and more intelligent, with the risk of humans ending up in a situation like gorillas - dominated by a smarter agent

842.label: 5-10 years

842.label: 5-10 years

Speed Limits: AI systems and their training protocols should have compute usage restrictions, including speed limits(measured in bit operations per second) to prevent getting out of control.

842.label: Restrictive regulation

Having an oversight body or committee broadly representative of the global public that would need to approve an AI project carrying a significant risk. Ensuring that we maintain the ability to gracefully shut down AI technology at a global scale, in case of emergencies caused by AI.

854.label: Long-term existential risk

However, once AI is a digital self-replicating, self-evolving system with its own evolutionary drivers and utility functions, and external substantiation in robotics, we will have a new sentient species to deal with.

871.label: Strongly support mandatory pre-deployment testing

871.label: Support advance risk identification

They should implement strong and enforceable safeguards, including requiring these companies to incorporate rights-respecting technical measures that block user attempts to produce these images.

931.cite: We are a non-regulatory agency, neutral and objective. Our job is to help industry develop technically sound, scientifically valid standards.

932.label: Supports regulatory oversight of AI market concentration
932.def: Believes AI presents similar competitive challenges to historical tech monopolies and requires antitrust enforcement to prevent market consolidation.
932.cite: I think AI may present many of the same kinds of questions and challenges. These are technologies that are built on massive datasets often.

932.label: Supports antitrust enforcement in AI
932.def: Views antitrust enforcement as necessary to ensure AI markets remain competitive and prevent monopoly control over critical infrastructure.
932.cite: If firms in the AI ecosystem violate the antitrust laws, the Antitrust Division will have something to say about it.

933.label: Targeted regulation
933.cite: I think the great news is the current administration is continuing to champion the idea of secure by design for software broadly. But she said 'the kicker' was that the recently released White House AI Action Plan talks specifically about cybersecurity and the need for AI systems that are created, designed, developed, tested, and delivered with security as the top priority.

940.def: Believes current statistical techniques will not lead to intelligence and that AGI concerns are a distraction
940.cite: I don't think AI is intelligent; nor do I think that the current (admittedly impressive) statistical techniques will lead to intelligence.

940.label: Manageable
940.def: Views AI risks as primarily economic and regulatory rather than existential, focusing on job displacement and market bubble risks
940.cite: I worry that AI is the asbestos we're shoveling into our civilization's walls, and our descendants will be digging it out for generations.

940.label: Targeted regulation

940.cite: The big question when we're talking about tech regulation is administrability. It's been the great defect of tech regulation to date.

944.label: Economic transition framework
944.def: AGI represents a structural economic phase transition where AI systems acquire autonomous planning, acting, and learning capabilities, shifting economic constraints from intelligence scarcity to trust and verification challenges.
944.cite: As this synthetic intelligence comes online, the semantic debate over whether it constitutes "Artificial General Intelligence" (AGI) is rapidly becoming a distraction. The exact threshold of human parity is economically and philosophically moot, because the specialization of these intelligences is fundamentally different.

944.def: AI poses existential risk that must be balanced against growth benefits through technological development and safety measures.
944.cite: Technological development raises consumption but may pose existential risk. A growing literature studies this tradeoff in static settings where stagnation is perfectly safe.

944.label: Targeted regulation
944.def: AI regulation that requires a balanced approach considering both benefits and risks of emerging technologies while exploring regulatory intricacies and their impact on democratic values.
944.cite: Regulating AI presents complex challenges that require a balanced approach, considering both the benefits and risks of emerging technologies. These papers explore the intricacies of AI regulation and its impact on democratic values and societal trust.

946.label: Serious risk
946.cite: The private sector is pursuing a path for generative AI that emphasizes automation and the displacement of labor, along with intrusive workplace surveillance. Simply displacing workers is never good for the labor market, even when the displaced are highly paid.

946.label: Serious risk
946.cite: While artificial intelligence is changing industries at an unprecedented pace, it also threatens job security, raises questions about fairness, and could deepen inequality.

946.label: Targeted regulation
946.cite: The first step in AI governance should be to ensure that current regulations apply to AI to the greatest extent possible. If a human activity without the use of AI is regulated, such as in health care and the law, then that activity combined with the use of AI should be similarly regulated.

946.label: Targeted regulation
946.cite: At this time, the five most important federal policies should be: 1. Equalize tax rates on employing workers and on owning equipment/ algorithms to level the playing field between people and machines. 2. Update Occupational Safety and Health Administration rules to create safeguards (i.e., limitations) on the surveillance of workers.

947.label: ASI definition
947.def: Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines
947.cite: The broad goal of U.S. AI policy should be to drive toward what we call Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines.

947.label: 10-25 years
947.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

947.label: Serious risks
947.cite: Companies claim they will achieve artificial general intelligence (AGI) within the decade, yet none scored above D in Existential Safety planning. One reviewer called this disconnect 'deeply disturbing,' noting that despite racing toward human-level AI, 'none of the companies has anything like a coherent, actionable plan' for ensuring such systems remain safe and controllable.

947.label: Targeted regulation
947.cite: Maintain a stable regulatory framework that ensures reliability, security, and legal compliance, leveraging existing sectoral regulations unless they prove insufficient for AI-specific risks.

948.label: ASI as policy goal
948.def: Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines
948.cite: The broad goal of U.S. AI policy should be to drive toward what we call Artificial Super Intelligence – a level of machine intelligence that surpasses the current state of the art in AI, going beyond statistics, data processing and pattern recognition toward deep reasoning, real-world understanding, and autonomous problem solving across disciplines.

948.label: High-stakes deployment requires enhanced safety
948.cite: As a general-purpose technology, AI systems will be deployed in high-stakes environments that require enhanced explainability and reliability beyond our current knowledge. Strategic investments in the explanation capabilities and reliability of AI systems will help maintain U.S. leadership in AI by supporting the design and deployment of more accurate systems.

948.label: Extend existing frameworks to AI
948.cite: If human activity without the use of AI is regulated, then the use of AI should similarly be regulated. The development, sale and use of AI systems should, whenever possible, be governed by the same standards and procedures as humans acting without AI in the AI's domains of applicability.

948.label: Leverage existing sectoral regulations
948.cite: Maintain a stable regulatory framework that ensures reliability, security, and legal compliance, leveraging existing sectoral regulations unless they prove insufficient for AI-specific risks. Many high-value, high-sensitivity applications of AI such as healthcare, transportation, and financial services already have stable regulatory requirements that apply to services both offered with or without AI.

949.label: Human-like versatile systems
949.def: General Artificial Intelligence (AGI) refers to AI systems with human-like versatility, capable of performing a wide range of tasks across various domains with adaptability and reasoning.
949.cite: General Artificial Intelligence (AGI) refers to AI systems with human-like versatility, capable of performing a wide range of tasks across various domains with adaptability and reasoning.

949.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

949.label: Global priority risks

949.label: Comprehensive risk assessment
949.cite: A new MITFutureTechproject reviewed 43 AI frameworks produced by research, industry and government organizations; they identified 777 risks in total.

949.label: Targeted regulation
949.cite: Maintain a stable regulatory framework that ensures reliability, security, and legal compliance, leveraging existing sectoral regulations unless they prove insufficient for AI-specific risks.

949.label: Values-based approach
949.cite: This project reimagines technology policy in K-12 schools, shifting from rigid, rule-based approaches to more flexible ones grounded in shared values.

950.label: Joint socio-technical property
950.def: AGI is not a property of software alone but a joint property of software and all the social, legal, technical, and institutional adaptations built around it.
950.cite: AGI is not a property of software. To ask whether an AI system is AGI is a category error. At best, AGI is a joint property—of software and all the social, legal, technical, and institutional adaptations we build around it.

950.label: Long-term process requiring decades
950.def: CITP views AGI implementation as requiring extensive social, legal, technical, and institutional adaptations that will take decades.
950.cite: Software alone isn't enough. To make AI work in the real world, we need to adapt industries, laws, infrastructure, norms. That's the real long-term process—and it's going to take decades.

950.label: Manageable with proper frameworks
950.def: CITP views AI risks as manageable through proper research, frameworks, and mitigation strategies rather than existential threats.
950.cite: Our work in this area involves identifying and mitigating emerging societal risks from AI and machine learning, and developing data science techniques to answer policy-relevant questions.

950.label: Supportive of clear regulations
950.def: CITP views regulation as providing certainty and preventing corruption when well-specified with limited discretion.
950.cite: There's a belief that the absence of regulation is freedom. It is, in a sense. In another sense, the absence of regulation is uncertainty. Where regulations aren't clear and well-specified, and there isn't a professional civil service, rules are applied inconsistently and the door is open to corruption.

950.label: Targeted regulation
950.def: CITP supports accountability mechanisms with multiple overlapping systems for AI regulation, focusing on fit-for-purpose assessments and standards-setting bodies.
950.cite: Our comment offers three core principles that the NTIA should take into account in developing recommendations for future regulation: First, the accountability ecosystem should have multiple, overlapping mechanisms for ensuring that AI systems are serving the public interest.

951.def: A nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better
951.cite: Synthesising a vast literature, we can say that AGI would be a nonbiological computational system that can perform any cognitive function currently performed by humans at the level of the median human or better.

951.label: Near-term AGI
951.cite: the people building the most advanced AI systems are explicitly and aggressively working to bring AGI about, and they think they'll get there in two to five years.

951.label: Existential threat to democracy
951.cite: In 2023, many loudly argued "no," not because of the implications for democracy, but because they considered AGI an existential threat to humanity at large. People have presented scenarios, ranging from speculative to compelling, in which AGI is humanity's final, civilization-ending invention.

951.label: Existential security threat
951.cite: The stakes are existential. Without coordinated, proactive intervention, AI's potential to manufacture consensus and manipulate societal systems will continue to pose an unprecedented challenge to global security and democratic institutions.

951.label: Targeted regulation
951.cite: AI governance should not be merely a reactive, punitive, status-quo-defending enterprise, but rather the expression of an expansive, proactive vision for technology—to advance human flourishing.

951.label: Public AI investment
951.cite: Public AI encompasses "publicly provided, -owned and -operated layers in the AI tech stack, such as cloud infrastructure, data, and model development; and public utility-style regulation of the private AI industry that fosters competition and prevents abuses of power."

952.label: Unknown
952.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

952.label: Serious
952.cite: Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

952.label: Targeted regulation
952.def: Rights-based regulatory approach applying existing laws across sectors to AI with targeted interventions where needed
952.cite: In the U.S. and in the African Union, regulatory agencies have found it more effective to apply existing laws —across data protection, competition, consumer protection, employment, and other sectors —to govern AI, often taking a sectoral approach.

952.label: Rights-based targeted approach
952.def: Building on existing civil rights frameworks with targeted AI-specific guidance for implementation
952.cite: We propose to build on the Blueprint, opening up the conversation between and across people working on AI governance from various fields.

953.label: Unknown

953.label: Manageable risks
953.def: AI risks are present but not as serious as existential threats, with focus needed on immediate harms rather than extinction scenarios.
953.cite: Professor Calo insists that the current narratives of AI are misguided and likely a distraction from on-going harms and risks. AI taking over might be possible, but discrimination, bias, accountability, and misinformation are already happening.

953.label: Targeted regulation
953.def: Focused on creating wiser, more inclusive tech policy through research and education rather than restrictive measures.
953.cite: The Lab's current priorities include providing guidance and context to the growing conversation on artificial intelligence, as well as work on tech policy in food resilience, which arrives in the wake of a pandemic and an intensifying climate crisis.

955.label: 5-10 years

955.label: Moderate

Korinek co-authored reports on frontier AI regulation and testified to US Senate on workforce preparation, supporting targeted interventions while emphasizing economic benefits.

956.label: AGI as hypothetical comprehensive AI
956.def: Roosevelt defines AGI as a hypothetical single AI technology that can do all human actions, which does not currently exist.
956.cite: Importantly, artificial general intelligence(AGI)—a hypothetical single AI technology that can do all human actions— does not currently exist.

956.label: Unknown
956.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

956.label: Serious financial system risks
956.def: Roosevelt views AI as posing serious risks to financial stability including potential crises, but focuses on specific harms rather than existential threats.
956.cite: AI agents pose new risks to the financial system, with the potential of sending it swinging from crisis to crisis. They may be used by malicious actors for fraud, market manipulation, and cyberattacks; can hallucinate and cause harm to financial institutions' customers; and can engage in herding behavior that results in bank runs or flash crashes.

956.label: Supports clear developer liability
956.def: AI developers should be held liable for harms caused by their systems, with clear legal frameworks assigning responsibility.
956.cite: Congress should act to ensure that the legal liability regime for AI agents is aligned with the public interest. This means allowing the users of AI agents and any other affected individuals to sue for damages when the agents have caused harm, notwithstanding agents' terms of service to the contrary.

956.label: Opposes restrictions that concentrate power
956.def: Open AI models and capabilities should be democratically accessible rather than concentrated among a few tech companies.
956.cite: Innovation happens quickest when a wave of different start-ups experiment with different approaches, rather than when concentrated in monopolies. Antitrust policy can play an important role in building a strong AI ecosystem that supports many start-ups with the capabilities they need to innovate.

956.label: Supports worker-centered evaluations
956.def: Pre-deployment testing should include worker input and evaluation to ensure AI serves worker and public interests.
956.cite: We advocate for the creation of roles such as AI impact monitors, experienced technologists who can act as partners to worker committees who audit and evaluate new technologies on an ongoing basis. This, in combination with mandates around meaningful transparency and strict prohibitions on automated firing and limitless surveillance, creates an advanced structure for workers to be true partners in developing their employers' technology procurement and implementation policies.

956.label: Corporate regulation and sectoral bargaining
956.def: Roosevelt supports a combination of corporate regulation and sectoral bargaining to address AI monopolization and ensure worker power.
956.cite: Regulators could mandate worker representation on managerial decision-making boards and require companies to take into account a wider swath of stakeholders. This would mean tech giants like Google, Meta, and OpenAI would need federal charters requiring them not only to put workers on the board but also to consider worker, community, and societal impacts of AI development— not just shareholder profits.

956.label: Targeted regulation with worker power focus
956.def: Roosevelt advocates for targeted AI regulation that centers worker voice and democratic participation in technology deployment decisions.
956.cite: The progressive answer to who makes the decisions about how AI is used in the workplace should be clear: workers themselves. Centering workers will lead to not only better outcomes for labor but more effective deployment of technology.

956.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
956.def: Federal preemption would prevent states from regulating AI and limit democratic participation in AI governance decisions.
956.cite: As progressives, it is our responsibility to refuse these constraints as we grapple with what could be some of the most consequential technological advances in a generation. We can envision a future in which all of us, not just a few tech billionaires and their investors, decide how to put advanced computational power to use and make the tough decisions about how we want to govern ourselves.

957.def: AI that is better than humans at all tasks, including all economically valuable work.
957.cite: As artificial intelligence progresses toward human-level capabilities, many of today's AI companies' goals are to create AI that is better than humans at all tasks, including all economically valuable work—something widely referred to as artificial general intelligence (AGI).

957.label: Near-term AGI
957.def: Expects widespread joblessness within current lifetime as AI becomes capable of performing economically valuable work
957.cite: If they succeed in developing human level Artificial Intelligence, it's likely that we'll see widespread and unprecedented joblessness within our lifetime, raising critical questions about economic stability and the future of human employment.

957.label: Near-term transformation
957.def: Expecting transformative AI capabilities within the next few years, with 2030 as a key timeframe for significant economic disruption.
957.cite: We are approaching a world where advanced AI functions less like a tool and more like a drop-in replacement for a white-collar worker—a shift that is no longer theoretical, but that researchers at leading AI labs have come to expect in the next few years.

957.label: Very near-term AGI
957.def: References 2030 scenarios (less than 4 years away) where advanced AI generates enormous wealth by taking over human tasks
957.cite: The Windfall scenarios shown in the graphic below focus on the year 2030, less than four years away.

957.label: Serious economic risks
957.def: Views AI as potentially causing widespread economic disruption requiring major institutional responses
957.cite: Artificial intelligence is poised to be one of the most significant economic disruptors in modern history — potentially on par with the Industrial Revolution or the rise of the internet.

957.label: Serious economic disruption
957.def: AI poses serious risks of widespread joblessness and economic disruption that could break existing paradigms and require new institutional responses.
957.cite: If they succeed in developing human level Artificial Intelligence, it's likely that we'll see widespread and unprecedented joblessness within our lifetime, raising critical questions about economic stability and the future of human employment.

957.label: Targeted intervention
957.def: Advocating for targeted policies including global dividend systems, Windfall Clauses, and international coordination to address AI-driven economic disruption without broad regulatory restrictions.
957.cite: Governments will need a concrete game plan. They will have to answer tough questions: How can the economic gains from AI be more broadly distributed? How should education systems evolve in response to an AI-driven economy?

958.label: Unknown
958.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

958.label: Society-scale consequences
958.cite: But the unknown risks of these new technologies mean that we shouldn't roll the dice by deploying widely and litigating harms over the next several decades.

958.label: Existential questions requiring collective input
958.cite: AI raises many impactful, complex, and even existential questions that shouldn't be answered by labs or governments alone.

958.label: Support democratizing model governance through open approaches
958.def: Open-source technologies as part of composable and diverse organizational structures that prevent power consolidation.
958.cite: The development of open-source technologies has shown the power of organizational stacks that are composable and dynamic. A key risk in the AI industry is power consolidation, as this skews both the incentives and market structure, in turn creating a more fragile ecosystem.

958.label: Support open-source approaches with democratic governance
958.def: Open-source models that enable community input and democratic governance rather than centralized control.
958.cite: While that project was tied to a closed model, the approach could work for any open-source model. So, over the past year we prototyped a replicable framework, Community Models, for any group to shape an AI model through the constitutional process.

958.label: Support evaluations with public input
958.def: Pre-deployment evaluations that incorporate collective input and public values into AI safety assessments.
958.cite: Our first pilot will focus on collective input into risks and evaluations for AI: what questions do people have about the impact of these technologies, and how do we evaluate based on those questions in particular?

958.label: Support evaluations for overreliance and post-deployment monitoring
958.def: Evaluations that assess risks like overreliance and include ongoing monitoring after deployment.
958.cite: Our recommendations, based on the findings, are: 1) Monitor post-deployment effects carefully. 2) Create evaluations for overreliance.

958.label: Democratic AI governance
958.cite: We founded the Collective Intelligence Project to find a new default path, and to build a better future. This work requires experimentation, commitment, resources, partnership, and coalition-building.

958.label: Public participation in AI decisions
958.cite: We need to do something different: incorporate collective input at the ground level, developing new ways to determine what is good and how to achieve it within the models themselves, and the control structures that govern them.

959.label: Unknown
959.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

959.label: Infrastructure Solutions
959.cite: Infrastructure does not stop superintelligence. It stops corporate concentration, automation bias, and single-point-of-failure risk: documented, addressable problems today.

959.label: Infrastructure over Regulation
959.cite: The government does not need to tell AI companies how to think. It needs to build the infrastructure that makes AI auditable, accountable, and interoperable.

959.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
959.def: Federal preemption would mean centralized AI rulemaking that could override state authority to protect public safety, privacy, and local needs.
959.cite: The battle between federal preemption and state autonomy in AI regulation encapsulates a broader question: how to foster rapid technological advancement while upholding public interest.

960.label: Near-term AGI
960.def: RadicalxChange refers to AI systems that can execute cognitive tasks better than humans and mentions AGI/ASI in the context of running out of training data.
960.cite: Indeed it is possible—perhaps in the next five or ten years—that AI systems will be able to execute any well-defined cognitive task better, faster, and cheaper than any human.

960.label: Serious risks
960.def: RadicalxChange focuses on economic disruption, power concentration, and societal transformation risks from AI.
960.cite: There is a lot of hype in AI, but as its capabilities grow it clearly will transform society. We intend to use the time we have now—the next several years—to build pragmatic systems of solidarity that preserve dignity and community against those transformations.

960.label: Targeted regulation
960.def: RadicalxChange advocates for specific regulatory frameworks for data intermediaries and collective bargaining rather than broad AI restrictions.
960.cite: Do not overreach with direct regulation of non-frontier AI…instead, develop a regulatory program that makes it simple and minimally legally risky to set up "trusted data intermediaries" (on the scale of communities, industries, and countries) which can act as collective bargaining agents in the marketplace.

961.label: Unknown

961.cite: 本部將配合本法之實施，積極推動以下工作：（一）建立 AI 風險分類框架：協助各目的事業主管機關進行風險識別、評估與應對，訂定以風險為基礎之管理規範。

961.label: Innovation-first approach
961.cite: The AI Basic Act's innovation-first statutory hierarchy (Article 11), its generous R&D exemptions (Article 17), and its reliance on industry self-regulatory guidelines (Article 16) are best understood not as regulatory failures but as expressions of a governance logic in which market facilitation takes precedence over ex ante risk regulation.

961.label: Innovation-friendly approach
961.cite: This act establishes seven principles for the government to promote the research, development and application of AI: sustainable development and well-being, human autonomy, privacy protection and data governance, cybersecurity and safety, transparency and explainability, fairness and non-discrimination, and accountability.

962.def: AGI should not be defined as systems that outperform humans at most economically valuable work, but rather as tools that enable new human capabilities rather than just replacing them
962.cite: Actually, I don't even like that definition of artificial general intelligence. The goal of machines should not be to just do what people do slightly better. Our tools are valuable to us because they allow us to do things we can't do.

962.label: 10-25 years
962.cite: With a 20-year lead time, a lot can happen with that. And I do think we have a 20-year lead time.

962.label: Serious risks
962.cite: The bad scenario is one where the value of human expertise is just liquidated very rapidly, and that would create enormous problems for society, and it wouldn't create problems of lack of material wealth. We'd have tons and tons of productivity and resources, but it would create a huge problem for distribution, for identity, and for our democracy.

962.label: Targeted regulation
962.cite: We should be directing where we want to go. We shouldn't treat the technology as autonomous. My friend, the philosopher Josh Cohen, once said, "The future's not just a prediction exercise, it's a design exercise." So we should be thinking about shaping where we want to direct the technology, not just adapting to what happens.

965.label: Human-level cognitive performance
965.def: AGI is defined as the ability to perform all cognitive tasks at least as well as skilled humans
965.cite: may reach the level of Artificial General Intelligence (AGI)—the ability to perform all cognitive tasks at least as well as skilled humans—and in the process, unlike any technology yet developed, could fully automate the process of research and development itself, including the creation of better AI systems.

965.label: 10-25 years
965.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

965.label: Manageable
965.cite: Submitted as "Manageable" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

965.label: FDA-like approval for regulated industries
965.cite: So I would, if I got to work on one policy area for AI, it would be to design an equivalence of the FDA (Food and Drug Administration) for every regulated industry that is going to be adopting AI. So let me explain what I mean and the reason for that would be to create markets, to have more AI, not less.

967.label: Narrow vs General AI distinction
967.def: AGI as technologies that can do all tasks better than humans, contrasted with ANI (Artificial Narrow Intelligence) which are good only at specific tasks
967.cite: Then there is what computer scientists and the sort of people you're describing are really interested in which is Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) which are in technologies that can't just do one or two tasks, but they can do all tasks, and they can do them better than we can.

967.label: Serious concern about AI impact
967.def: AI as a transformative technology requiring careful consideration of its societal implications
967.cite: Big tech companies, with superb engineers and almost unlimited resources, tell us they are about to build a system that can outperform us at everything that we do. Yes, of course there are sensible reasons to treat those claims with skepticism. But we should also be asking the obvious question: what happens if they succeed?

967.label: Focused concern on narrow AI impacts
967.def: Risk from multiple specialized AI systems rather than single AGI system
967.cite: I suppose one of the arguments that I make in my book, and in my work more generally, is that what worries me in thinking about the future is actually less sort of one giant AGI, Artificial General Intelligence, and more a sort of army of smaller ANIs.

967.label: Moderate
967.cite: Advocates for thoughtful policy responses to AI's labor market impacts, including consideration of Universal Basic Income and Job Guarantee Schemes. Emphasizes need for governments to prepare for AI-driven economic transformation rather than restrictive regulation of AI development itself.

968.cite: Engage in serious competition enforcement in the area of AI right now, and do it pretty vigorously so that we don't end up in a bad spot in 5 or 10 years.

969.label: Transformative AI

969.cite: This data is revolutionizing prediction, creating AI systems that promise to handle complex tasks that humans cannot, given our more limited processing power. advanced AI is expected to outperform humans in an expanding range of intellectual and information-processing tasks.
