969.label: Manageable with policy
969.cite: If we get the policy response right, we do not need to fear a world with TAI.

969.label: Manage pace of change
969.cite: the goal of policy should be to shape the pace and direction of technological change, slowing the adoption of technologies that reduce jobs so that these changes happen on a timeline that works with human capacity to adapt.

969.label: Targeted regulation
969.cite: we have to address as a society the problems with ownership, competition, and regulation of AI.

970.label: 5-10 years
970.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

970.label: Serious
970.cite: Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

970.label: Targeted
970.cite: Advocates for targeted regulation addressing AI's labor market impacts through safety nets and competition policy, proposing a two-tier system with AI Adjustment Insurance and Digital Dividend rather than broad AI restrictions.

974.label: 10-25 years
974.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

974.label: Manageable
974.cite: Submitted as "Manageable" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

974.label: Targeted
974.cite: Advocates for application-specific AI regulation through existing agencies rather than federal-level blanket rules, emphasizing competition policy and merger enforcement over broad restrictions. Supports pro-competitive regulation including codes of conduct for dominant platforms and data mobility requirements.

975.def: AI today is not artificial general intelligence (AGI) – it's prediction technology
975.cite: The first idea is that AI today is not artificial general intelligence (AGI) – it's prediction technology.

975.label: System transformation timeline
975.def: Timeline for AI to have transformational system-level impact on society
975.cite: The core idea of Power and Prediction is that AI is an exciting technology – but it's going to take time to see its effects, because a lot of complementary innovation has to happen as well. And part of our agenda in the book is to accelerate the timeline of this innovation from 40 years to 10, or even less.

975.label: Manageable with proper preparation
975.cite: So there's lots of different proposals on the table. Right now it's not obvious that we need new tools, but to the extent we're worried about disruption, we need to make sure that the social safety net tools that we do have are robust. There is a real risk in the short term of labor market disruption, particularly in certain industries.

975.label: Targeted regulation
975.cite: A lot of the challenges to AI adoption come from ambiguity about what's allowed or not in terms of regulation. The regulatory resistance to change, and the ambiguity of what's allowed, is a real barrier. As we start thinking about system changes, there is an important role for government through legislation and regulation, as well as through its coordinating function as the country's biggest buyer of stuff, to help push us toward new AI-based systems.

976.def: AGI is AI capable of performing the full breadth of human intellectual tasks, including any intellectual task that humans can such as learning to drive, fly a plane, or write a Ph.D. thesis.
976.cite: Most of the public thinks of AGI as AI that is as intelligent as people, and one useful definition is AI that can perform any intellectual task a human can.

976.label: Decades away
976.def: Ng defines true AGI as AI capable of performing the full breadth of human intellectual tasks, such as learning to drive, fly a plane, or write a Ph.D. thesis.
976.cite: He says true AGI—that is, AI capable of performing the full breadth of human intellectual tasks—remains decades away.

976.label: Decades away
976.def: Ng uses the definition that AGI should be able to do 'any intellectual tasks that a human can' including learning to drive a car, fly a plane, or write a Ph.D. thesis.
976.cite: Andrew Ng, a leading AI researcher, said in a recent interview with Techsauce that AGI should be able to do "any intellectual tasks that a human can." According to Ng, though, we're still decades away from seeing anything close to that.

976.label: Vastly overhyped
976.def: Ng sees AI risks, particularly extinction risk, as vastly overstated and sensationalist, though he acknowledges some legitimate risks in specific applications.
976.cite: I have spoken with many of the leading voices who are worried about AI catastrophic risks, including extinction risk. In the case of extinction risk, I just don't get it — I don't see any plausible path for AI to lead to human extinction.

976.label: Light-touch regulation
976.def: Ng advocates for regulating AI applications (not the technology itself) with a tiered approach based on risk and reach, opposing burdensome requirements on open-source AI or general AI models.
976.cite: However, it is important to differentiate regulating applications (which we need) vs. regulating the technology (which is ill-advised).

976.label: Light-touch regulation
976.def: Ng opposes burdensome licensing requirements and sees current regulatory landscape as more harmful than helpful due to lobbying by large tech companies to stifle open-source competition.
976.cite: THERE HAS BEEN A LOT OF FEAR ABOUT AI POSTING IN EXISTENTIAL THREAT BUT I DON'T GET IT. I DON'T SEE ANY PLAUSIBLE WAY THAT IT COULD MAKE HUMANITY EXTINCT. THIS CREATES COVER FOR REGULATORS TO PASS LAWS THAT WILL BE STIFLING ON OPEN SOURCE.

977.def: General intelligence as machines that can do everything a human can and more, distinguished from today's highly specialized AI systems that lack common sense, contextual understanding, and flexible reasoning.
977.cite: I think it's important to distinguish between general intelligence as we imagine it in science fiction – machines that can do everything a human can and more – and of what today's AI systems are capable.

977.label: Not close, highly specialized
977.cite: Despite rapid progress, we are not close to achieving true artificial general intelligence. Even the most powerful current models are highly specialised. They excel at pattern recognition, language modeling, or planning in well-defined environments. But they lack common sense, contextual understanding, and the flexible reasoning that even a child demonstrates.

977.label: Serious but manageable
977.cite: The systems we do have are increasingly influential, and they can already behave in ways that are surprising, opaque, and sometimes risky when deployed at scale, especially in high-stakes applications like healthcare, law enforcement, or autonomous systems. So, while I'm not worried about machines "waking up," I am deeply invested in how we design, deploy, and govern increasingly capable AI.

977.label: Targeted regulation
977.cite: From a systems perspective, we need transparent, accountable, and explainable AI, especially when it is embedded in physical systems. At the same time, policies around data governance, labor impacts, and access to AI infrastructure are crucial to ensuring that benefits are felt widely.

977.label: Stable targeted framework
977.cite: Maintain a stable regulatory framework that ensures AI reliability, security, and legal compliance, leveraging existing sectoral regulations wherever possible.

979.label: Unknown
979.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

979.label: Societal disruption risk
979.def: AI poses risks of economic disruption and creative industry collapse without proper regulation
979.cite: If this thing is allowed to sort of run away, without regulation and without compensation to those whose work it's using, it basically is a new dark age. He said that AI 'pre-stages' a new dark age and could result in the 'complete evisceration of incentives to create knowledge in many fields.'

979.label: Pro-regulation
979.def: Favors regulatory intervention to prevent AI harms, drawing on social media regulation precedent
979.cite: There's really a precedent in social media for regulation, and doing it sooner rather than later. Pasquale said that when social media first came about, people didn't really anticipate 'how badly it could be misused and weaponized by bad actors.'

982.def: Technology that can meet and match humans on just about any task
982.cite: Not just an Age of AI, where machines and software, like Siri, perform specific and narrow tasks, but an Age of Artificial General Intelligence, in which technology can meet and match humans on just about any task.

982.label: 5-10 years
982.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

982.label: Serious risks requiring governance
982.cite: We are simply ill-prepared for the impact of yet another massive social transformation. We should avoid rushing into all of this with only a few engineers at a small number of labs setting the direction for all of humanity.

982.label: Moderate regulation
982.cite: I joined a long list of technologists, academics and even controversial visionaries such as Elon Musk in signing an open letter calling for a pause for at least six months of 'the training of AI systems more powerful than GPT-4.'

984.label: Conditional/Uncertain
984.cite: Let's assume, for example, that AI kind of plateaus because of technological limitations and ends up simply being a decent tool for writing rough drafts of various kinds of things.

984.label: Manageable
984.cite: Submitted as "Manageable" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

984.label: Light-touch regulation
984.cite: AI presents a mix of issues. For instance, if I'm hit by an AI-assisted driver, I'd sue the driver and possibly the manufacturer. The same legal rules would apply.

985.label: 25+ years or never
985.cite: Submitted as "25+ years or never" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

985.label: Mixed/nuanced
985.cite: Submitted as "Mixed/nuanced" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

985.label: Targeted
985.cite: Advocates for treating AI as a social technology requiring complex governance reforms rather than simple restrictions, emphasizing the need for interdisciplinary approaches that combine social science with computer science to address AI's integration into existing institutions like markets, bureaucracy, and democracy.

986.label: Unknown
986.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

986.label: Manageable with governance
986.def: AI risks can be managed through proper democratic governance structures and human oversight, though current institutional safeguards are inadequate.
986.cite: I do worry that we didn't put in place the dams, know, the institutional checks and balances and the governance structure we need to channel that sort of technological breakthrough for the greater good rather than the enrichment of a few people at the top.

986.label: Targeted regulation
986.def: AI governance requires not just expert intervention but more citizen voice and democratic deliberation, with regulation better enforced through AI-empowered democracies.
986.cite: Like other ethical and political challenges, such as gene editing, AI governance requires not just more expert intervention and regulation but more citizen voice and input—for example, on how to navigate the distributive impact of AI on the economy.

989.def: Superintelligence already exists in collective systems humanity has created—markets, democracies, corporations, religious communities
989.cite: Glen Weyl, researcher and economist at Microsoft and co-founder of the Plurality Institute, does not think superintelligence is something we are waiting to build. He argues that it already exists—in the collective systems humanity has created, from markets and democracies to corporations and religious communities.

989.label: 5-10 years
989.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

989.label: Manageable with design
989.cite: Glen Weyl, political economist at Microsoft Research and founder of RadicalxChange, argues that AI could be used to create more participatory, decentralized, and democratic systems, if we design it right.

989.label: Targeted intervention
989.cite: Can we secure the opportunities these technologies offer without destroying the social fabric or imposing authoritarian control?

990.label: Flexible and general intelligence
990.def: AGI is a shorthand for any intelligence that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence
990.cite: I take AGI to be "a shorthand for any intelligence ... that is flexible and general, with resourcefulness and reliability comparable to (or beyond) human intelligence".

990.label: Skeptical of near-term AGI
990.cite: Overall, then, the AI 2027 scenario almost certainly underestimates how much time we have to prepare for general intelligence, by years if not decades.

990.label: AGI not imminent
990.cite: We won't get to AGI in 2026 (or 7). At this point I doubt many people would publicly disagree, but just a few months ago the world was rather different.

990.label: Threat to democracy
990.cite: I don't think the current AI we have is an existential threat but that it is dangerous. In many ways, I think it's a threat to democracy.

990.label: Catastrophic but not extinction
990.def: By "catastrophe", Marcus means an event in which AI contributes to the deaths of 1% of the global population through escalation of conflict, bioweapons, or systemic misinformation.
990.cite: Extinction is pretty unlikely. The possibility of catastrophe is fairly high – and the probability of dystopia is quickly approaching 100%.

990.label: FDA-style oversight
990.cite: Maybe you want to have some kind of licensing around things that are going to be deployed at very large scale, but they carry particular risks, including security risks.

990.label: Restrictive regulation
990.cite: That's exactly why I wrote the book: to encourage citizens to take action, maybe even boycott GenAI until it gets its house in order.

992.label: Multiple intelligences
992.def: Intelligence is not a single capacity but a collection of distinct cognitive modes: exploration (learning about the world), exploitation (acting effectively toward goals), and care (helping other agents achieve their goals).
992.cite: Gopnik identifies three distinct types of intelligence: exploration (learning about the world), exploitation (acting effectively toward goals), and care (helping other agents achieve their goals).

992.label: 25+ years or never
992.cite: Submitted as "25+ years or never" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

992.label: Manageable with proper regulation
992.cite: I think it's fair to say that the consensus among people who study human intelligence is that there's a much bigger gap between human and artificial intelligence, and that the real risks we should pay attention to are not the far-off existential risks of AI agents taking over but rather the more mundane risks of misinformation and other bad stuff showing up on the internet.

992.label: Moderate regulation needed
992.cite: But we still have to put a lot of work into developing norms and regulations to deal with AI systems. An example I like to give is, imagine that it was 1880 and someone said, all right, we have this thing, electricity, that we know burns things down, and I think what we should do is put it in everybody's houses.

993.label: Augmented collective intelligence over AGI
993.def: Prefers moving from artificial general intelligence to augmented collective intelligence, where AI amplifies rather than replaces human agency.
993.cite: A better tech future moves from artificial general intelligence to augmented collective intelligence, where AI amplifies rather than replaces human agency.

993.label: 5-10 years
993.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

993.label: Serious risks requiring proactive management
993.def: AI poses serious risks that require proactive management, particularly around frontier models with dangerous capabilities that could pose severe risks to public safety.
993.cite: Advanced AI models hold the promise of tremendous benefits for humanity, but society needs to proactively manage the accompanying risks. Frontier AI models pose a distinct regulatory challenge: dangerous capabilities can arise unexpectedly; it is difficult to robustly prevent a deployed model from being misused; and, it is difficult to stop a model's capabilities from proliferating broadly.

993.label: Targeted regulation with public input
993.def: AI regulation should involve public participation and collective intelligence mechanisms rather than closed development or pure industry self-regulation.
993.cite: We need to build civic infrastructure now, create leverage points in AI development for public input, develop tools that make participation as diverse and responsive as possible, and prove repeatedly that collective intelligence produces better outcomes than closed development.

994.label: Rejects singular AGI concept
994.cite: When I was appointed as Taiwan's Minister for Digital Affairs in 2016, I wrote the following short poem to describe my job... When we hear "the singularity maybe near", let us remember: the plurality is here.

994.label: Serious risks requiring attention
994.cite: I want the entire world to know that we currently know almost nothing about the actual shape of AI risks, and which is why I signed, in a way, to basically say that this is a global priority alongside the other two, which we know much more about.

994.label: Targeted regulation
994.cite: At the bare minimum, there needs to be undeniable common knowledge so that a country can see emerging harms and simply say, "Okay, this is committing what's called epistemic injustice. It is colonizing our way of relating, of being. And so unless a foreign model stops causing such harm, or even better, if a foreign model can help to repair some of those harms, we're not going to let it enter into our work stream, into our education stream, or any other stream."

994.label: Targeted regulation
994.cite: While tech elites argue over whether AI development should accelerate or slow down, Tang stresses that the steering wheel must be handed to the people most affected by AI.

996.label: Supports multilateral compute oversight
996.def: A multilateral compute oversight system that monitors large AI clusters and training runs above compute thresholds like 10^26 FLOP.
996.cite: The EU should start international dialogues to implement a multilateral compute oversight system that builds on the monitoring requirements of the EU AI Act and the recent US Executive Order 14110.

996.label: Supports accountability measures
996.def: Democratic governance and accountability mechanisms for AI systems rather than corporate self-regulation.
996.cite: What's crucial is the principle that AI must be governed, and I want that governance to be democratic. I don't want to live in a society where corporations make decisions about our lives and societies instead.

996.label: Mixed on restrictions
996.def: Open foundation models are models released with widely available weights, which relinquishes developer control over downstream use.
996.cite: Therefore, liability for arising from downstream usage could chill the to severe liability risk models are conjectured to to malicious uses of AI, the weakness of evidence is striking.

996.label: Supports mandatory evaluations
996.def: Pre-deployment testing and evaluations including red-teaming, capability evals, and safety assessments as required by EU AI Act.
996.cite: Schaake served as Chair of the Working Group on Internal Risk Management and Governance of General-Purpose AI Providers for the Code of Practice, with a focus on AI model safety and security.

1003.label: Economic replacement
1003.def: The ability for an AI to replace human beings behind a screen - if the economy can't tell that we swapped out a human with an AI, that's what AGI is.
1003.cite: you can still broadly understand AGI as the ability for an AI to replace human beings behind a screen. That is, if the economy can't tell that we swapped out a human with an AI, well, that's what AGI is.

1003.label: 2-3 years
1003.cite: Submitted as "2-3 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1003.label: Civilizational risk
1003.cite: Imagine you're about to get on an airplane and 50% of the engineers that built the airplane say there's a 10% chance that their plane might crash and kill everyone.

1003.label: Existential threat
1003.cite: And currently we are releasing the most powerful, inscrutable, uncontrollable, omni-use technology that we've ever invented. One that's already demonstrating the kind of self-preservation, deception, and escape blackmail behaviors we previously thought only exist in sci-fi movies. And we're deploying it faster than we've deployed any other technology in history, under the maximum incentives to cut corners on safety. To me, that sounds like an existential threat.

1003.label: Targeted coordination
1003.cite: In practical terms, Aza points to measures such as restricting or regulating AI companions for minors, treating certain AI interactions similarly to regulated domains like therapy, and ensuring that systems are designed to support human well-being rather than undermine it.

1003.label: International coordination
1003.cite: Think of it like the nuclear test ban treaty. We got all the nations together saying can we agree we don't want to deploy nukes above ground.

1004.label: Unknown
1004.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1004.label: Serious current harms
1004.cite: While it is a good model in that it requires platforms to take down content, it doesn't scratch the surface of the many privacy, economic and national security risks A.I. poses.

1004.label: Opposition to deregulation
1004.cite: Sen. Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) called the order "the wrong approach — and most likely illegal," arguing that it strips away some of the few existing protections Americans have against AI harms.

1004.label: Moderate federal regulation
1004.cite: Senator John Thune and I have come together to lead a bill that would promote innovation and transparency for A.I. systems in high-risk settings such as health care or public safety.

1004.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
1004.def: Federal preemption means using federal power to override state AI laws before federal standards are established.
1004.cite: But we can't supersede state protections until we have strong, enforceable federal standards in place. Tech companies should not be allowed to use their lobbying power to undo the few protections Americans have from the downsides of A.I. — passed at the state level with bipartisan support.

1005.label: Strong supporter of tightening controls
1005.def: Closing loopholes in export controls on AI chips and extending them to remote access scenarios to prevent adversaries from accessing controlled technology.
1005.cite: The remote-access loophole is a major weakness in America's national security policy. Keeping cutting-edge chips out of the hands of China, Russia and other unfriendly regimes is key to securing U.S. AI leadership and global economic competitiveness.

1005.label: Opposes weakening controls on China
1005.def: Maintaining robust export controls on semiconductor chips and AI technology to China rather than using them as bargaining chips in trade negotiations.
1005.cite: Robust and stable export controls are essential to protect American national security and global technological leadership. We urge you to reconsider this misguided strategy and ensure that our nation's export controls are no longer used as a bargaining chip.

1005.label: Opposes extending Section 230 to AI
1005.def: Section 230 protections should not apply to generative AI outputs since AI systems produce content rather than merely hosting third-party content.
1005.cite: Cox and Wyden drafted Section 230 together in 1996—and they're skeptical that its protections apply to generative AI.

1006.label: Strongly supports expanding controls
1006.def: Supports legislation requiring presumption of denial for all entities in China and Russia.
1006.cite: U.S. Senators Roger Wicker, R-Miss., and Marco Rubio, R-Fla., introduced the Depriving Enemy Nations of Integral Authorizations and Licenses (DENIAL) Act of 2023 to require BIS to adopt a presumption of denial for any end user from China or Russia.

1006.label: Strongly supports tighter controls
1006.def: Favors presumption of denial for all tech exports to China and closing circumvention loopholes.
1006.cite: The Commerce Department should adopt a blanket 'presumption of denial' posture for export license applications that would send critical technology to any entity based in China, according to Sen. Marco Rubio (R-Fla).

1006.label: Supports targeted liability reforms
1006.def: Favors removing Section 230 protections for dominant platforms using algorithms to manipulate content.
1006.cite: By eliminating legal protections for market-dominant tech firms that engage in censorship or use algorithms to manipulate their users' choices, it would enable Americans to sue such firms for provable harm.

1006.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
1006.def: Opposes state AI laws that create regulatory burden on industry, preferring minimal federal oversight.
1006.cite: Sen. Marco Rubio (R-FL) is potentially open to supporting regulations on how the federal government uses AI, but he's opposed to Congress regulating Silicon Valley firms who sell Americans private data like it's a commodity.

1007.label: Economic Value Definition
1007.def: AGI was a system you could go to that can do any economically valuable task at human performance or better
1007.cite: When people talk about AI and the original AGI and how we spoke about it when OpenAI started, AGI was a system you could go to that can do any economically valuable task at human performance or better. That was the definition.

1007.label: Decade Timeline
1007.cite: I feel like the problems are tractable, they're surmountable, but they're still difficult. If I just average it out, it just feels like a decade to me.

1007.label: March of Nines
1007.cite: The cost of failure in self-driving is extraordinarily high. This creates the 'march of nines' problem: 90% working = impressive demo, 99% working = still dangerous, 99.9% working = maybe usable with supervision.

1007.label: Keep AI on a Leash
1007.cite: I'm still the bottleneck. I have to make sure this thing isn't introducing bugs, he asserted.

1009.label: Human-level cognitive capabilities
1009.def: AGI refers to software that's capable of learning any task or subject, able to do everything that a human brain can do, but without any practical limits on the size of its memory or the speed at which it operates.
1009.cite: By contrast, the term artificial general intelligence refers to software that's capable of learning any task or subject. AGI doesn't exist yet—there is a robust debate going on in the computing industry about how to create it, and whether it can even be created at all.

1009.label: Disagrees with decade timeline
1009.cite: Bill Gates says we're likely to get 'two more turns of the crank' on building bigger large language models, but that scale alone won't deliver AGI.

1009.label: Focused on immediate over existential risks
1009.cite: The best reason to believe that we can manage the risks is that we have done it before.

1009.label: Skeptical of pausing AI development
1009.cite: I don't think asking one particular group to pause solves the challenges. Clearly there's huge benefits to these things… what we need to do is identify the tricky areas.

1012.label: 2-3 years
1012.cite: Submitted as "2-3 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1012.label: Catastrophic
1012.cite: Submitted as "Catastrophic" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1012.label: Restrictive
1012.cite: Strongly advocates for strict federal regulation of AI to prevent mass job displacement, warning that 'AI is going to wipe out 50% of American jobs overnight' within five years. Demands union contracts include language protecting workers from automation and calls for federal laws preserving human jobs over AI replacement. Supports the AFL-CIO's 'Workers First Initiative on AI' calling for transparency, human oversight, and prohibition of AI surveillance tools for union-busting.

1016.label: Ill-defined
1016.cite: Submitted as "Ill-defined" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1016.label: Catastrophic risks
1016.def: Humans First views AI risks as catastrophic, warning of threats to societal stability, national security, economic prosperity, civil liberties, privacy, and democratic governance
1016.cite: This race to replace poses risks to societal stability, national security, economic prosperity, civil liberties, privacy, and democratic governance. It also imperils the human experiences of childhood and family, faith, and community.

1016.label: Strongly supports developer liability
1016.def: AI developers and deployers should bear legal liability for defects, misrepresentation, and inadequate safety controls.
1016.cite: Developer Liability: Developers and deployers bear legal liability for defects, misrepresentation of capabilities, and inadequate safety controls, with statutes of limitation that account for harms emerging over time.

1016.label: Opposes restrictions on open weights
1016.def: Open-weight models should remain accessible for transparency, democratic accountability, and to prevent concentration of AI power.
1016.cite: The Humans First movement and allied advocacy groups have pushed for mandatory disclosure requirements for AI systems with societal-scale deployment across healthcare, legal, education, and government procurement sectors.

1016.label: Strongly supports mandatory pre-deployment testing
1016.def: AI systems must undergo rigorous pre-deployment testing for safety risks and harmful impacts before public release.
1016.cite: Pre-Deployment Safety Testing: Like drugs, chatbots must undergo pre-deployment testing for increased suicidal ideation, exacerbation of mental health disorders, escalation of acute crisis situations, and other known harms.

1016.label: Restrictive regulation
1016.def: Humans First advocates for restrictive AI regulation through prohibition of superintelligence development, mandatory oversight, liability frameworks, and democratic control over AI deployment
1016.cite: Development of superintelligence should be prohibited until there is broad scientific consensus that it can be done safely and controllably, there is strong public buy-in. Independent Oversight: Highly autonomous AI systems where controllability is not obvious require pre-development review and independent oversight: genuine authority to understand, prohibit, and override, not industry self-regulation.

1016.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
1016.def: Federal preemption means preventing states from creating or enforcing their own AI laws and regulations.
1016.cite: Join representatives from the Center for American Progress, the AFL-CIO Tech Institute, the Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights, Public Citizen, and Public Knowledge for a discussion about why state preemption of AI, child safety, and privacy laws is not the path forward for meaningful AI legislation.

1017.label: Unknown
1017.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1017.label: AI poses serious risks to children and families
1017.def: AI risks are viewed as serious enough to warrant immediate policy intervention and safeguards
1017.cite: If we let AI run wild, our future becomes a sea of grieving families, suicide notes, online predators strip-mining the emotional health of our children, robbing them of their innocence for profit

1017.label: Strongly opposes restrictions on open weights
1017.def: Open source development means making AI model weights freely available for download and adaptation by developers.
1017.cite: Machine learning is a classic example of upfront research and development costs. Incentivizing open-source development increases efficiency in training by reducing double-spending.

1017.label: Pro-safeguards regulation
1017.def: The organization believes in advancing AI with guardrails and safeguards to protect children, workers, and families while maintaining innovation
1017.cite: The American people and conservative voters demand real and strong guardrails and protections that balance corporate power with liability and responsibility.

1017.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
1017.def: Federal preemption refers to preventing states from creating AI-specific laws while Congress develops a unified national framework.
1017.cite: Alliance for the Future strongly supports this provision, and we thank Chairman Brett Guthrie (KY-02) for his leadership in moving this language through the committee.

1018.label: Unknown
1018.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1018.label: Serious
1018.cite: Submitted as "Serious" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1018.label: Restrictive
1018.cite: Advocates for strict restrictions on AI military applications including bans on autonomous weapons and mass surveillance, demands transparency in AI corporate governance, and opposes AI industry influence in politics through campaign donations and lobbying.

1018.label: Support federal preemption
1018.def: Federal preemption of state AI laws through executive order directing FTC to classify state-level AI bias laws as federally preempted and conditioning federal funding on states repealing AI regulations.
1018.cite: Trump's executive order directs the FTC to preempt state AI laws and conditions $42B in federal funding on states repealing AI regulations.

1039.label: Oppose compute thresholds
1039.def: Compute thresholds like the 10^26 FLOP requirement for AI transparency reporting constitute burdensome regulation that should be eliminated.
1039.cite: The Trump administration last week moved to block a Utah AI transparency bill that would require the largest artificial intelligence developers to publish safety plans... targeting companies that have used at least 10²⁶ integer or floating-point operations (FLOPs) to train a model.

1039.label: Support closing loopholes
1039.def: Export controls on AI chips and manufacturing equipment to China should be tightened to close loopholes that allow adversaries to access critical technology.
1039.cite: President Trump and House Republicans are determined to prevent China from leapfrogging the United States... That's why I'm introducing the STRIDE Act to close carveouts in U.S. export controls, allowing allied toolmakers to fuel China's military.

1039.label: Support limited liability with transparency
1039.def: AI developers should be protected from lawsuits if they meet disclosure requirements, with liability remaining with professionals who use AI tools.
1039.cite: Sen. Cynthia Lummis, R-Wyo., is introducing legislation Thursday that would shield artificial intelligence developers from an array of civil liability lawsuits provided they meet certain disclosure requirements.

1039.label: Oppose restrictions on open weights
1039.def: Open weight AI models are the best way to prevent left-wing bias in models while promoting innovation and competition.
1039.cite: Vance has also defended open weight AI models as the best way to prevent left-wing bias in models, while dismissing their risks.

1039.label: Strongly oppose mandatory testing
1039.def: Pre-deployment safety evaluations should be voluntary rather than mandatory federal requirements that hinder AI innovation.
1039.cite: Elimination of Pre-Deployment Testing Previous federal guidelines required AI systems above certain capability thresholds to undergo safety evaluations. These are now 'strongly encouraged' but entirely voluntary.

1039.label: Strongly support federal preemption
1039.def: Federal preemption prevents fragmented state-by-state regulatory landscape that hinders innovation and creates conflicting AI compliance burdens.
1039.cite: House Republican leaders are pressing ahead on a proposal to preempt state-level AI regulation, two GOP aides told Axios on Thursday.

1039.label: Strongly support preemption with exceptions
1039.def: Congress must create 'one federal rulebook for AI' to address fragmented state-by-state regulatory landscape while preserving some state authority for child protection and consumer laws.
1039.cite: In announcing draft framework, Blackburn said Congress must create 'one federal rulebook for AI' to address what she described as a fragmented state-by-state regulatory landscape that is hindering innovation.

1040.label: Strongly support tighter controls
1040.def: Export controls refer to restrictions on selling advanced AI chips to China and requiring congressional approval for such sales.
1040.cite: For years, the Chinese Communist Party has treated America's cutting-edge chips like an all-you-can-eat buffet, fueling surveillance, military modernization, and influence campaigns. This bill puts an end to that practice.

1040.label: Strongly support developer liability
1040.def: Developer liability refers to holding AI companies legally responsible when their AI systems cause harm to users.
1040.cite: Big Tech's time to police itself is over. By opening the courtroom and allowing victims to sue, our bill will force AI companies to develop their products with safety in mind.

1040.label: Support federal testing capacity
1040.def: Pre-deployment testing refers to government capacity to test and evaluate AI systems before deployment to ensure safety and security.
1040.cite: As Majority Leader, I have kept up the drumbeat that our government must implement smart guardrails to make sure that we balance the need for the U.S. to continue to lead in innovation while also addressing any potential risks posed by artificial intelligence.

1040.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1040.def: Federal preemption refers to federal laws that would override or prohibit state AI regulations and common law remedies.
1040.cite: Committee Democrats underscored that federal preemption of all state common law, statutes, and regulations regarding AI would create a dangerous, unregulated environment, deny families a path to hold companies accountable, hinder AI innovation, and even paralyze future federal action.

1040.label: Strongly oppose preemption moratorium
1040.def: AI preemption refers to legislation that would impose a moratorium on state-level AI regulations.
1040.cite: Following a survey of its members, the Congressional Progressive Caucus announced its official position to oppose the attachment of legislative language being considered by Republicans that would preempt or impose a moratorium on state-level regulations on artificial intelligence (AI) in the National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA).

1051.label: Strongly support AI product liability
1051.def: AI systems should be treated as products subject to liability standards, holding developers responsible for harms caused by unsafe design or inadequate warnings.
1051.cite: CHT has long believed that high-risk AI systems should be considered products, and that liability standards are essential for creating an ecosystem where innovation and responsibility work in partnership in tech development, rather than in opposition.

1051.label: Support mandatory testing with independent oversight
1051.def: Pre-deployment safety testing should be rigorous, ongoing, and conducted by independent oversight rather than allowing AI companies to grade their own homework.
1051.cite: We need independent oversight so that we know these products are safe before they're released. And this is just not the case in this industry despite being the case in many other consequential industries.

1051.label: Strongly oppose 10-year moratorium
1051.def: A 10-year moratorium would prevent states from addressing AI-related issues including child safety, deepfakes, and fraud while federal standards are being developed.
1051.cite: CHT firmly opposes this moratorium due to its length and broad scope. We strongly urge Congress not to hinder the states' ability to protect their citizens from harmful AI products while federal standards are being developed.

1051.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1051.def: Federal preemption prevents states from regulating AI while providing no alternative federal framework, creating a vacuum of accountability.
1051.cite: Yesterday, the Trump administration released an Executive Order that seeks to preempt state-level AI legislation. This Order poses a threat to public safety by impeding on states' abilities to regulate AI in any meaningful capacity.

1052.label: Strongly support developer accountability
1052.def: Builders and deployers of AI systems should be held directly accountable for harms caused by their products.
1052.cite: The onus of creating tools that are safe to use should be on the companies that build and deploy generative systems, which means that builders of these systems should be made accountable for the outputs produced by their products.

1052.label: Support open weights models
1052.def: Open weights models as alternatives to monopolistic Big Tech approaches that enable decentralized, community-rooted AI development.
1052.cite: My idea was to have a federation of these small organizations where they share data amongst themselves, but externally, there's like a kind of an easier interaction between them.

1052.label: Support transparency and accountability
1052.def: Mandatory transparency, documentation and accountability for AI systems before deployment to prevent harms.
1052.cite: What we need is regulation that enforces transparency. Not only should it always be clear when we are encountering synthetic media, but organizations building these systems should also be required to document and disclose the training data and model architectures.

1052.label: Oppose federal preemption
1052.def: Federal preemption viewed as removing state protections while providing no binding federal replacement, creating a regulatory void.
1052.cite: Timnit Gebru is the founder and executive director of the Distributed AI Research (DAIR) Institute, an independent organization conducting community-rooted research.

1061.label: Support developer liability
1061.def: Liability frameworks should ensure developers and deployers of high-risk AI tools can be held accountable when their systems cause harm or violate consumer protection laws.
1061.cite: Liability frameworks that ensure developers and deployers of high-risk AI tools can be held accountable – not just users employing AI for harm – when their systems cause harm or violate consumer protection laws.

1061.label: Support developer liability
1061.def: Liability frameworks should hold platforms and developers accountable when their AI systems harm children, not just users employing AI for harm.
1061.cite: Congress should establish liability frameworks to hold platforms accountable when their AI systems harm children.

1061.label: Support mandatory evaluations
1061.def: Mandatory pre-deployment testing includes rigorous risk-based assessments and safety testing, particularly for platforms accessible to minors.
1061.cite: Companies should be required to undergo mandatory safety testing and transparent reporting of AI safety failures, particularly for platforms accessible to minors.

1061.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1061.def: Common Sense Media views federal preemption as preventing states from enforcing AI laws that protect families and children from AI risks and harms.
1061.cite: Common Sense Media is disappointed in the ruling by the parliamentarian to allow a 10-year moratorium on AI state law enforcement within the budget reconciliation bill, a provision opposed by bipartisan lawmakers, state attorneys general, civil society groups including SafeAI Now (safeainow.org), and Americans across the country.

1061.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1061.def: Federal preemption would prevent states from protecting against high-impact AI and invalidate key state laws, including protections for civil rights and children's privacy.
1061.cite: We, the undersigned organizations committed to protecting civil rights, promoting consumer protections, and fostering responsible innovation, write to express our strong opposition to a provision in the House Energy and Commerce Committee Draft Budget Resolution, passed out of committee on May 14, that would preempt state and local laws governing artificial intelligence (AI).

1062.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1062.def: Federal preemption refers to efforts to override state authority to regulate AI through congressional moratoriums or executive agency actions.
1062.cite: On behalf of the National Conference of State Legislatures (NCSL), the bipartisan organization representing the legislatures of our nation's states, territories and commonwealths, we write to reaffirm our opposition to any federal proposal that preempts state authority to legislate or regulate artificial intelligence (AI).

1063.label: Supports developer liability
1063.def: Algorithmic management creates an accountability gap where employers can distance themselves from disciplinary decisions by claiming 'the system flagged you' rather than taking direct responsibility.
1063.cite: An algorithm that generates a discipline recommendation distances the company from the decision in ways that reduce both legal liability and reputational exposure. 'The system flagged you' is not the same as 'I decided to discipline you' — even if the former is a consequence of choices made by the latter.

1063.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption
1063.def: Federal preemption would strip communities of the ability to safeguard workers and the general public against harmful and unregulated AI use.
1063.cite: This federal preemption provision would override state and local authority, stripping our communities of the ability to safeguard workers and the general public against harmful and unregulated AI use. It should be stripped from any final bill.

1067.label: Skeptical of compute thresholds
1067.def: CFR views compute-based export controls and thresholds as strategically problematic when applied to adversaries like China.
1067.cite: The rule inadvertently demonstrates that there is no version of an AI chip export policy to China that is simultaneously permissive, implementable, enforceable, and protective of US national security. The most effective policy remains the simplest one: denying the export of all AI chips to China.

1067.label: Strongly support tight controls
1067.def: CFR supports maintaining strict export controls on AI chips to adversaries and opposes loosening restrictions.
1067.cite: The strategic case for selling advanced AI chips to China has always been weak: modest commercial gains for a handful of US chip companies against substantial risks of enabling Chinese military AI capabilities, eroding US technological leadership, and increasing Chinese competition for every other U.S. technology company.

1067.label: Support industry-led testing
1067.def: CFR supports mandatory pre-deployment evaluations but prefers industry-led coalition approaches over government mandates.
1067.cite: Industry leaders like Anthropic, Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and OpenAI—a strategic cohort that has been responsibly transparent and repeatedly explicit about the compounding AI crisis of control—must form a coalition of the willing to lead their industry forward with shared principles, clear reporting requirements, common testing protocols, and uniform security practices.

1067.label: Mixed/fragmented approach
1067.def: CFR views AI governance as requiring a balance between federal agency oversight using existing authorities and avoiding fragmented regulatory environments abroad.
1067.cite: Despite the hype surrounding the technology, AI systems are just tools that help people and organizations perform tasks, and most of the areas where these tools could be deployed already fall under the jurisdiction of one or more government regulators. As such, agencies may not need new legal powers to regulate AI in many cases—their existing authorities can likely do the trick just fine.

1068.label: Support risk-weighted compute governance
1068.def: WEF views compute thresholds as a measurable basis for risk-weighted governance similar to carbon markets.
1068.cite: Risk-weighted compute permits will not solve every problem. But they can help the world move from slogans to scalable incentives at exactly the moment when AI capability and infrastructure are accelerating together.

1068.label: Support clear accountability frameworks
1068.def: WEF views liability as requiring clearly assigned responsibilities across the AI supply chain with appropriate remedy provisions.
1068.cite: Maintaining accountability and oversight for trustworthy digital technologies requires clearly assigned and well-defined legal responsibilities alongside remedy provisions for upholding individual and social expectations.

1068.label: Balanced approach needed
1068.def: WEF sees open weights as beneficial for innovation and transparency but requiring governance frameworks to manage risks.
1068.cite: By embracing a distributed, hybrid architecture, societies can capture far greater economic value while simultaneously reducing strategic dependency.

1068.label: Strongly support comprehensive evaluations
1068.def: WEF defines evaluation as comprehensive assessment including task success rates, reliability, behavioral patterns, and contextual testing in real-world conditions.
1068.cite: Evaluation involves examining dimensions such as task success rates, reliability of tool use, performance, behavioral patterns over time, user trust, interaction patterns, and operational robustness within real-world workflows.

1068.label: Oppose fragmented governance
1068.def: The WEF views state-level fragmentation as creating a deficit of trust and hindering global cooperation on AI governance.
1068.cite: Global AI governance would help nations to align on sovereignty, interoperability and inclusion for the benefit of all. This fragmented development will have a significant effect on trust in AI and on whether it can benefit people around the world.

1099.label: Supportive of compute-based AI dominance strategy
1099.def: Computing power thresholds and restrictions to maintain U.S. AI dominance over adversaries like China.
1099.cite: Winning the AI race will be critical to our national security and economic prosperity for decades to come. We must do everything to protect American AI dominance from adversaries like Communist China.

1099.label: Strong supporter of tighter AI chip export controls
1099.def: Controls on advanced AI chips with location verification mechanisms to prevent diversion to adversaries like China.
1099.cite: With these enhanced security measures, we can continue to expand access to U.S. technology without compromising our national security.

1101.cite: Idaho and America are at the leading edge of semiconductor innovation. It is vital we maintain this position by strengthening our export controls and closing loopholes that our adversaries exploit to obtain critical technologies like semiconductor manufacturing equipment.

1101.label: Supports AI testing systems

1101.cite: The Idaho National Laboratory is already a leader in national security and cybersecurity, and the TEST AI Act will rightly use the National Lab's capabilities to establish safeguards and systems to prevent misuse of this growing technology.

1108.label: Support compute-based governance mechanisms
1108.def: Compute thresholds are regulatory triggers based on floating point operations (FLOPs) used during AI model training to determine oversight requirements.
1108.cite: Development costs and compute are compelling metrics for use in AI governance because they 'correlate with capabilities and risks, are quantifiable, can be measured early in the AI lifecycle, and can be verified by external actors.'

1108.label: Strongly support chip export controls
1108.def: Export controls on AI chips and semiconductor manufacturing equipment to prevent adversaries from accessing technologies critical for military AI applications.
1108.cite: This requires robust measures to protect critical assets, including adaptable export controls targeting hardware (advanced semiconductors, manufacturing equipment) and potentially, explicitly denying strategic competitors access to parity.

1108.label: Support developer accountability
1108.def: Developer liability means holding AI companies responsible for harms caused by their systems through existing legal frameworks and accountability mechanisms.
1108.cite: Yet, in different ways and without new legislation, countries already have legal structures to hold companies liable for the consequences of products they develop and deploy.

1108.label: Mixed on open-weight restrictions

1108.cite: This involves developing clear policies regarding the use of open-weight models originating from strategic competitors like the PRC, potentially restricting their deployment in sensitive government systems or critical infrastructure due to security, data provenance, and embedded alignment concerns.

1108.label: Support mandatory evaluations for consequential AI
1108.def: Pre-deployment testing involves conducting safety evaluations and capability assessments before releasing AI systems that could have significant societal impact.
1108.cite: A critical AI governance principle is that U.S. regulators cannot regulate every AI use case. Rather, the U.S. regulatory approach must focus their efforts on highly consequential AI use cases, whether beneficial or harmful.

1108.label: Support federal preemption for innovation
1108.def: Federal preemption should be used to prevent state regulatory overreach that hinders AI innovation and development.
1108.cite: In contrast, our federalist system provides a dynamic advantage as states serve as laboratories for targeted AI governance, and overreach can be thwarted with preemption.

1118.label: Strongly support preemption to protect innovation

1118.cite: There's still an interest in making sure that you don't have states like California that wreck the ability to innovate in artificial intelligence, similar to what Europe did to wreck their innovation.

1168.def: Federal preemption would prevent states from regulating AI impacts on families and children, which Cox views as a states' rights issue.
1168.cite: I'm very worried about any type of federal incursion into states' abilities to regulate AI. We've seen how social media companies, the most powerful companies in the history of the world, have used this incredible tool to utterly destroy our kids and our families, their mental health, to use this in ways that have made them a lot of money and gotten people addicted to outrage.

1169.def: A system capable of doing almost any cognitive task a human can do, with systems that can replace human beings in cognitively demanding jobs
1169.cite: A canonical definition of A.G.I. is a system capable of doing almost any cognitive task a human can do. I don't know that we'll quite see that in the next four years or so, but I do think we'll see something like that, where the breadth of the system is remarkable but also its depth, its capacity to, in some cases, exceed human capabilities, regardless of the cognitive discipline — Systems that can replace human beings in cognitively demanding jobs.

1192.label: Human cognitive matching
1192.def: IBM defines AGI as a hypothetical stage where an artificial intelligence system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across any task
1192.cite: IBM gives one of the more succinct and straightforward definitions of AGI: 'Artificial general intelligence is a hypothetical stage in the development of machine learning in which an artificial intelligence system can match or exceed the cognitive abilities of human beings across any task'.

1192.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1192.label: Serious emerging threats
1192.def: IBM identifies frontier AI models and agentic AI as creating new categories of enterprise threat that are fast moving, systemic and increasingly autonomous
1192.cite: Frontier models are creating a new category of enterprise threat that is fast moving, systemic and increasingly autonomous. Meeting that threat requires a systemic defense. AI powered offense demands AI powered defense.

1192.label: Serious risks requiring management
1192.def: IBM views AI risks as serious enough to require comprehensive risk management frameworks, governance structures, and multiple layers of safeguards
1192.cite: Despite implementing robust, internal AI safety and governance measures, there will always be risk. Effective risk mitigation does not mean zero risk. Ideally, users should take a 'Swiss cheese model' of safety, adopting multiple layers of safeguards in their deployed AI systems, each with different 'holes.'

1192.label: Targeted regulation
1192.def: IBM advocates for precision regulation approach to AI that regulates AI risk and applications rather than algorithms themselves
1192.cite: IBM applauds EU negotiators for reaching a provisional agreement on the world's first comprehensive AI legislation. We have long urged the EU to take a carefully balanced approach, focused on regulating high-risk applications of AI while promoting transparency, explainability, and safety among all AI models.

1192.label: Precision regulation
1192.def: IBM advocates for precision regulation that focuses on regulating AI risk rather than algorithms, making creators and deployers accountable, and supporting open AI innovation
1192.cite: For years, we have advocated for a precision regulation approach to AI, which our CEO expanded upon in recommendations for advancing trusted AI: regulating AI risk and not AI algorithms; making AI creators and deployers accountable, not immune to liability; and supporting open AI innovation, not an AI licensing regime.

1194.label: Skeptical of AGI concept
1194.def: AGI as a coherent concept is questioned - seen potentially as an artifact of how humans categorize cognition rather than a meaningful technical milestone
1194.cite: Not everyone agrees with these predictions. Ali Farhadi, the CEO of the Allen Institute for Artificial Intelligence, told*The New York Times*: "I'm all for projections and forecasts, but this [AI 2027] forecast doesn't seem to be grounded in scientific evidence, or the reality of how things are evolving in AI."

1194.label: Manageable with proper measures
1194.cite: Artificial intelligence is becoming more deeply embedded in our world, enabling all sorts of new possibilities —but also raising new risks and dangers as well. Data privacy, embedded biases, intentional and unintentional false information are all major concerns.

1194.label: Risk-based licensing
1194.cite: The license restrictions are risk-based and therefore, artifact agnostic. Because licenses rely on risk assessment and aren't segmented by artifact or artifact type (the data and the models to be licensed), they can be readily applied to any derivative downstream models and applications. To do this, the risk categories of the licenses —low, medium, and high —are assigned based on an assessment completed by a multidisciplinary group of lawyers, ethicists, and scientists.

1196.def: AGI is where computers are more powerful than people
1196.cite: There's absolutely no probability that you're going to see this so-called AGI, where computers are more powerful than people, in the next 12 months.

1196.label: Decades away
1196.cite: There's absolutely no probability that you're going to see this so-called AGI, where computers are more powerful than people, in the next 12 months. It's going to take years, if not many decades, but I still think the time to focus on safety is now.

1196.label: Serious risks requiring action
1196.cite: AI is a huge tool. Unfortunately, in this case, it's become weaponised.

1196.label: Moderate regulation
1196.cite: I do think we would benefit from a new agency, a new licensing system, something that would ensure not only that these models are developed safely, but they're deployed in, say, large data centers, where they can be protected from cybersecurity, physical security and national security threats.

1196.label: Moderate regulation
1196.cite: The only defence that will work is a collective defence that relies on a partnership between governments and trusted companies.

1203.label: Embodied intelligence required
1203.def: Human intelligence involves embodied experience, cultural context, and neurosymbolic understanding that current AI systems fundamentally lack, not just computational power plus data
1203.cite: In my opinion, the fundamental misunderstanding across these four categories is a reductive view of intelligence itself. Human intelligence isn't just computational power plus data, it's not just being good at math or chess or chemistry, but it involves embodied experience, cultural context, and neurosymbolic understanding that current AI systems fundamentally lack.

1203.label: Long-term skeptic
1203.def: Views most near-term AGI predictions as hype driven by incentives rather than technical reality
1203.cite: When we filter out voices influenced by these four motivations, we find remarkably few credible experts still making bold AGI claims. Those without these incentives who work directly with AI systems tend to speak more cautiously about capabilities and timelines, focusing instead on present limitations.

1203.label: Present-day harms focus
1203.def: Views current AI systems as causing serious real-world harms that require immediate attention over speculative future risks
1203.cite: By understanding the constellation of incentives behind AGI claims, we can better evaluate which voices to trust and which predictions deserve our skepticism. More importantly, we can refocus attention on addressing real, present-day challenges posed by existing AI systems rather than being distracted by speculative futures.

1203.label: Targeted child protection
1203.def: Supports specific regulations protecting children from AI harms while preserving state authority
1203.cite: States can require companies offering AI products to minors to maintain specific levels of liability coverage, and when insurers refuse to cover a product because the risk is unquantifiable, that refusal becomes objective evidence of an unsafe product.

1203.label: Moderate regulation advocate
1203.def: Supports liability frameworks and targeted state-level regulations while maintaining innovation space
1203.cite: Congress and state legislatures must act to make clear that tech companies have a clear duty to exercise reasonable care in the design of their products. This duty forms the core of legal liability that every other successful American industry abides by.

That's because we've been closely watching the development of AI products over the last few years, and could tell — these products are not being rolled out safely.

1204.label: Targeted regulation

1204.cite: At CHT, we see product safety — and the common-sense regulation that supports it — as a prerequisite for true innovation. With the right incentives, companies are motivated to put the needs of their users first — leading to better, more reliable products.

This framework builds on the Executive Order issued on December 11, 2025, Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, which directed the Department of Justice, the Federal Trade Commission, the Federal Communications Commission, and the Department of Commerce to evaluate and, where necessary, challenge state AI requirements deemed inconsistent with a 'minimally burdensome' national standard.

1260.label: Comprehensive ethical framework
1260.def: Moderate regulatory approach emphasizing human-rights-based development with multi-stakeholder governance and adaptive oversight mechanisms
1260.cite: The governance framework established by the Recommendation is centered on the principle of multi-stakeholder collaboration, emphasizing that no single entity—be it a government or a corporation—can manage the ethical implications of AI in isolation. It calls for the establishment of independent, multi-disciplinary, and multi-stakeholder ethical impact assessment mechanisms and oversight bodies.

1261.cite: Gill considers this moment as our narrow shot to avoid another Cold War-style arms race with AI. "In the mid-1950s—the London talks when Eisenhower was the president and Bulganin was the leader on the Soviet side—there was a very interesting opportunity that was missed to prevent an arms race that took off. Then you had the Cuban Missile Crisis, and the world came close to the brink of nuclear war," he says. "Why not be more thoughtful about this today?"

1261.label: Serious risks requiring governance

1261.label: Supportive of oversight mechanisms
1261.def: Gill views compute-based governance as part of updating traditional governance tools to ensure accountability for rapidly developing AI technologies.
1261.cite: So how do you respond? By updating the toolkit and by better leveraging a mix of soft and hard norms, because negotiating hard norms is tough these days.

1261.label: Supportive of safety evaluations
1261.def: Gill views pre-deployment testing as part of necessary guardrails and safety measures to ensure AI serves humanity's interests before deployment.
1261.cite: Most importantly, effective guardrails are required to ensure that A.I. serves humanity's interests, fostering inclusion and safety globally.

1261.label: Targeted regulation
1261.cite: Therefore, Gill argues that A.I. governance must balance innovation with trust and safety. When innovation undermines these core principles, it not only jeopardizes individual rights but also risks disrupting the social contract.

1261.label: Targeted regulation with international cooperation

1295.label: Statutory definition
1295.def: OMB uses the statutory definition from the McCain NDAA which includes artificial systems that perform tasks under varying circumstances, solve tasks requiring human-like cognition, or are designed to think or act like humans
1295.cite: The term "artificial intelligence" has the meaning provided in Section 238(g) of the John S. McCain National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2019, which states that "the term 'artificial intelligence' includes the following": 1. Any artificial system that performs tasks under varying and unpredictable circumstances without significant human oversight, or that can learn from experience and improve performance when exposed to data sets. 2. An artificial system developed in computer software, physical hardware, or other context that solves tasks requiring human-like perception, cognition, planning, learning, communication, or physical action.

1304.label: Supports with reorganization
1304.def: AI chip export controls involve restricting technology transfers to adversaries, but enforcement approach can be restructured.
1304.cite: On February 5, 2025, in one of her first actions as the nation's chief law enforcement official, Attorney General Pam Bondi issued several new memoranda that significantly refocus US Department of Justice (DOJ) national security investigations, resources, and enforcement priorities.

1304.label: Actively enforcing preemption
1304.def: Federal preemption involves challenging state AI laws that are deemed inconsistent with federal policy or that regulate interstate commerce.
1304.cite: The memo from Attorney General Pam Bondi cites President Trump's executive order last month aimed at restricting "excessive" state AI rules. The memo said that the AI Litigation Taskforce will challenge state laws on grounds that they are illegal, unconstitutionally regulate interstate commerce or are overridden by existing federal regulations.

1304.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
1304.def: Federal preemption means establishing a single national standard rather than allowing 50 different state AI regulatory approaches.
1304.cite: President Donald Trump's AI executive order issued Thursday compels Bondi to "establish an AI Litigation Task Force (Task Force) whose sole responsibility shall be to challenge State AI laws inconsistent with the policy set forth in section 2 of this order, including on grounds that such laws unconstitutionally regulate interstate commerce, are preempted by existing Federal regulations, or are otherwise unlawful in the Attorney General's judgment."

1306.label: Strongly opposes compute-based regulation
1306.def: Compute thresholds and government reporting requirements represent heavy regulation that could slow down AI innovation.
1306.cite: Ultimately, we just want to make sure we don't have government getting in the way of the innovation that's happening. But no new heavy regulations and billions of dollars of government money to do what's already being done in the private sector.

1306.label: Supports framework approach
1306.def: Pre-deployment evaluations as part of a national AI framework that provides certainty while protecting consumers.
1306.cite: House Republicans look forward to working across the aisle to enact a national framework that unleashes the full potential of AI, cements the U.S. as the global leader, and provides important protections for American families.

1306.label: Strongly supports federal preemption
1306.def: Federal preemption prevents states like California from creating AI laws that could wreck innovation and create a patchwork of limitations.
1306.cite: There's still an interest in making sure that you don't have states like California that wreck the ability to innovate in artificial intelligence, similar to what Europe did to wreck their innovation.

1321.label: Responsible AI framework
1321.cite: Our approach is grounded in four simple but powerful principles. Every piece of AI we develop is: Human-centered Our AI solutions are built on a foundation of human-centered principles that puts humans in control of AI-based decisions. Inclusive Our solutions are constantly tested to promote fairness for all and to minimize bias. Transparent We communicate with our customers transparently on the topic of AI using clear, understandable terms. Accountable Trust is the cornerstone of our AI initiatives. We have adopted an oversight structure to provide accountability and governance.

1321.label: Moderate governance
1321.cite: AI governance provides the framework that allows organizations to harness AI's full potential while maintaining control, accountability, and ethical responsibility. By establishing policies, oversight processes, and technical controls, organizations can ensure that AI systems operate reliably and align with organizational values and regulatory requirements.

1322.label: Serious safety concerns
1322.def: Canada treats AI safety risks seriously enough to demand direct access to AI company protocols and require reporting of serious incidents, particularly those involving public safety threats.
1322.cite: Minister of Artificial Intelligence Evan Solomon announced that the AI Safety Institute has been granted access to OpenAI's protocols. This move comes after it was revealed that OpenAI had banned the shooter, Jesse Van Rootselaar, from using its services due to concerning interactions but had not notified law enforcement.

1322.label: Serious risks requiring oversight
1322.def: Canada views AI risks as serious enough to warrant the creation of dedicated safety institutes, mandatory reporting of serious incidents, and close government oversight of AI company practices.
1322.cite: the launch of the Safe and Secure Artificial Intelligence Advisory Group to advise the government on the risks associated with AI systems and ways to address them

1322.label: Risk-based approach
1322.def: Canada promotes a risk-based approach that distinguishes between low-risk and higher-risk AI uses, encouraging experimentation within controlled environments while requiring stronger safeguards for systems that could affect legal rights.
1322.cite: The Guide distinguishes between low‑risk uses (for example internal drafting, brainstorming, research assistance) and higher‑risk uses such as public‑facing chatbots or systems that summarize client data or inform administrative decisions. It promotes a risk‑based approach, encourages experimentation within controlled environments, and advises institutions to limit use to contexts where risks can be effectively managed.

1322.label: Targeted regulation
1322.def: Canada's approach focuses specifically on high-impact AI systems that pose significant risks to health, safety, human rights, or economic interests, while avoiding stifling innovation in less risky areas.
1322.cite: AIDA would establish a regulatory framework for high-impact AI systems in Canada, requiring responsible design, development, and deployment practices. The legislation introduces requirements for impact assessments, transparency, monitoring, and accountability measures for AI systems that pose significant risks to health, safety, human rights, or economic interests.

1356.label: Serious governance risks
1356.def: AI poses serious risks to democratic governance and public sector autonomy if not properly managed, particularly regarding dependency on private sector systems.
1356.cite: But there is a glimmer of a good question inside of this whole affair: Who controls the technology that the government depends on, and how do we ensure adequate protection of the public's interests when that technology is fundamentally opaque? This has been one of the most important governance questions of the last decade and will only become more important in the next one.

1356.label: Manageable with proper governance
1356.def: AI risks are real but manageable through proper institutional design, governance frameworks, and careful implementation rather than requiring dramatic restrictions.
1356.cite: To navigate this moment effectively, government should focus less on the novelty of AI and more on the institutional choices that will shape its long-term impact. That shift in perspective reveals a set of practical risks that have little to do with model architectures and everything to do with how the public sector acquires, governs, and deploys technology.

1356.label: Pragmatic middle path
1356.def: Niskanen Center supports a pragmatic approach to AI governance that avoids both extreme positions of treating AI as existential threat or transformative revolution.
1356.cite: Public debate about AI often polarizes around future hypotheticals — on one side, AI as an existential threat; on the other, AI as an inevitable, world-transforming revolution. Both frames focus on risks and capabilities that are not yet present in available systems. In reality, there is a pragmatic middle path that addresses today's challenges: strengthening institutions, improving decision making capacity, and integrating AI only where it can reliably support public missions rather than distort them.

1356.label: Targeted regulation
1356.def: Niskanen Center advocates for 'right-sizing' AI guardrails and targeted regulatory approaches that focus on specific risks rather than blanket restrictions.
1356.cite: It is reasonable to order an agency to study potential harmful effects of a new policy or technology, such as AI, but the existing accumulation of bureaucratic procedures mean such studies can take years to complete. Too often, the risk of inaction is not weighed against the risk of action.

1356.label: Opposes federal preemption
1356.def: Federal preemption would eliminate state authority to regulate artificial intelligence and impose a moratorium on state AI laws.
1356.cite: A moratorium on state AI laws, of any length, would undermine these efforts and deny Americans the benefits of state-led experimentation and progress.

1357.label: Catastrophic AI/nuclear risks

1357.label: Serious risks requiring management
1357.cite: Managing misuse risks in dual-use foundation models is of paramount importance given their broad applicability and potential for harmful use.

1357.def: FAS does not take a clear position on compute-based regulatory thresholds but discusses their use in various policy frameworks without explicit endorsement or opposition.
1357.cite: Development costs and compute are compelling metrics for use in AI governance because they correlate with capabilities and risks, are quantifiable, can be measured early in the AI lifecycle, and can be verified by external actors.

1357.label: Supports strengthening chip export controls

1357.cite: A future 301 investigation could almost certainly find a way to prohibit goods with Chinese-made semiconductors from entering the U.S. market.

1357.label: Supports enhanced AI developer accountability
1357.def: FAS supports holding AI developers responsible for their systems' outputs and behavior, requiring disclosure of AI use and validation of AI-generated content.
1357.cite: Authors alone are responsible for all AI errors, omissions, or data hallucinations (i.e., manufactured data and/or 'facts').

1357.label: Supports open source AI development
1357.def: FAS supports joint development of open-source AI tools and collaborative access to advanced AI models through public-private partnerships.
1357.cite: These partnerships could encompass co-funded research, shared datasets and computing resources, collaborative access to advanced AI models, and joint development of open-source tools.

1357.label: Strongly supports mandatory pre-deployment evaluations
1357.def: FAS defines mandatory pre-deployment testing as comprehensive risk assessments, red-teaming, and safety evaluations that must be conducted before AI system deployment.
1357.cite: Before launching a frontier AI system, developers must ensure safety, trustworthiness, and reliability through pre-deployment risk assessment.

1357.label: Multi-layered governance

1357.label: Supports state authority with federal coordination

1357.label: Strongly opposes federal preemption

1357.cite: While a well-crafted federal framework may ultimately be the right path forward, preempting all state regulation in the absence of federal action would leave a dangerous vacuum, further undermining public confidence in these technologies.

Overbroad rules could eliminate the open innovation that will be essential for transparency, competition, and national resilience in AI.
