This race risks eroding efforts to ensure AI systems are accurate, reliable, safe, secure, and protect basic rights.

1525.label: Targeted regulation
1525.cite: This approach focuses on supporting actors in advocating for increased governance over the technology sector as well as ensuring human, civil rights, and other public interest considerations are central in the governance of technology.

1525.label: Targeted regulation
1525.cite: The stakes are too high to defer decisions to a handful of companies and leaders within them.

1525.label: Targeted regulation
1525.cite: That's a very different orientation than 'How do we invest to make the LLMs be the biggest and fastest,' right? It's an orientation that centers opportunity and young people.

1528.label: Support liability protections for performers
1528.def: AMPTP supports requiring consent and compensation when using a performer's likeness to generate new performances through AI.
1528.cite: The AMPTP agrees with SAG-AFTRA that use of a performer's likeness to generate a new performance requires consent and compensation.

1528.label: Support mandatory consent requirements
1528.def: AMPTP requires advance, specific consent from performers and written consent with description of intended use before creating digital replicas.
1528.cite: Advance, specific consent from the performer required both to create and use Digital Replicas. No Digital Replica of the performer can be created without the performer's written consent and description of the intended use in the film.

1547.def: AGI defined as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult, grounded in Cattell-Horn-Carroll theory with ten core cognitive domains including reasoning, memory, and perception
1547.cite: The lack of a concrete definition for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) obscures the gap between today's specialized AI and human-level cognition. This paper introduces a quantifiable framework to address this, defining AGI as matching the cognitive versatility and proficiency of a well-educated adult.

1547.label: 5-10 years
1547.cite: Submitted as "5-10 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1547.label: Existential risk
1547.cite: Let me say first of all, by 'doom' I mean something like, by 2100, the world is in a state where I can see that almost everything that I value has been destroyed. Maybe we're not literally dead, but we've been forced to be uploaded in some very unfavourable conditions, where it's just like some crappy lossy copy that never gets run.

1547.label: Targeted governance
1547.cite: One of the big things I say is what we need to do is upgrade our sense making and governance and forecasting and coordination mechanisms. All of these things need to be much better and more reliable before the writing is too much on the wall that 'there's no alpha in humans' and 'don't listen to humans' and we lose de facto power.

1563.label: Open-ended continual learning with theory of mind
1563.def: AGI requires open-ended continual learning and reflective theory of mind - the capacity to understand oneself and one's own learning process.
1563.cite: If you have a general intelligence, it is necessarily capable of open ended continual learning. And the most difficult, challenging intellectual problem there is, reflective theory of mind. What do I know? You know, he knows that she knows. And literally to work on AGI is to work on the capacity at least for alignment and vice versa.

1563.label: 4 to 40 years (90% confidence)
1563.cite: Timeline-wise for general AI, if you wanted me to give a 90% confidence interval, I'm like four to 40 years. If someone thinks they have a narrower confidence interval than that, I would like to know why they think they know so much about this thing that no one's built yet.

1563.label: Substantial chance kills us all
1563.cite: I would say I am probably most aligned with the AI will become human-level intelligence and there's a substantial chance, if we do it wrong, that it kills us all, and that's very bad.

1563.label: Could destroy all life
1563.cite: "That kind of intelligence is just an intrinsically very dangerous thing," he added. "Because intelligence is power." An AI doomsday scenario would be "like a universe-destroying bomb" that wipes out all life, Shear said.

1563.label: Pause near human-level
1563.cite: "If we're at a speed of 10 right now, a pause is reducing to 0. I think we should aim for a 1-2 instead." "The way you make it safely through a dangerous jungle at night is not to sprint forward at full speed, nor to refuse to proceed forward. You poke your way forward, carefully," he said.

1563.label: Too early for regulation
1563.cite: Apart from concerns that AI could destroy all life, Shear thinks it's too early for regulation when it comes to concerns of AI disrupting industries or enforcing discrimination. "There probably will be a good regulation to write, we don't know what it looks like. The area is evolving so fast, it's very hard to write good regulation," he told Bartlett.

1571.label: Support compute thresholds for AI governance
1571.def: RAND supports compute-based thresholds as practical tools for AI governance, particularly the 10^26 FLOP threshold used in regulations.
1571.cite: Development costs and compute are compelling metrics for use in AI governance because they correlate with capabilities and risks, are quantifiable, can be measured early in the AI lifecycle, and can be verified by external actors.

1571.label: Support strategic chip export controls
1571.def: RAND supports sophisticated export controls on AI chips to China that maintain U.S. technological advantages while preventing Chinese companies from switching to domestic alternatives.
1571.cite: A smart approach to export controls would focus on setting a performance threshold for AI chips that can be sold to China based on a window between U.S. and Chinese hardware capabilities. The performance threshold should be high enough to outperform China's domestic hardware options to ensure Chinese developers remain on U.S. platforms.

1571.label: Neutral analysis of existing liability framework
1571.def: RAND analyzes how existing tort law applies to AI developers without advocating for specific liability reforms, noting that current law already creates significant liability exposure.
1571.cite: AI developers face considerable liability exposure under U.S. tort law for harms caused by their models, particularly if those models are developed or released without utilizing rigorous safety procedures and industry-leading safety practices.

1571.label: Support security measures for model weights
1571.def: RAND focuses on securing frontier AI model weights from theft and misuse, particularly for models that could pose national security risks.
1571.cite: Where most studies have focused on the security of AI systems more broadly, this study focuses on the potential theft and misuse of foundation AI model weights—the learnable parameters derived by training the model on massive data sets—and details how promising security measures can be adapted specifically for model weights.

1571.label: Support mandatory pre-deployment evaluations
1571.def: RAND supports mandatory safety evaluations, red-teaming, and risk assessments for AI systems before deployment, particularly for high-risk models.
1571.cite: Ensure that U.S. AI efforts conduct risk assessments prior to model training, as well as safety evaluations and red team tests prior to model deployment.

1571.label: Neutral/Technical Analysis
1571.def: RAND does not take a direct position on federal preemption, but provides technical analysis on governance approaches without advocating for specific preemption policies.
1571.cite: The researchers identified several areas in which government involvement could provide unique value: establishing AI security standards, facilitating intelligence- and information-sharing, and expanding developers' access to government penetration testing and personnel vetting programs.

1585.label: Industrial revolution-scale impact
1585.def: AI systems that would, if widely available, result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution, or AI that can substitute for humans in scientific endeavors.
1585.cite: Another study attempts to predict when AI systems that would, if widely available, result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution—their model estimated that such an outcome is 50% likely to occur by 2033.

1585.label: 2033 median estimate
1585.def: Sevilla refers to AGI as AI systems that would result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution, and separately as AI that can substitute for humans in scientific endeavors.
1585.cite: Another study attempts to predict when AI systems that would, if widely available, result in societal changes comparable in magnitude to the industrial revolution—their model estimated that such an outcome is 50% likely to occur by 2033.

1585.label: Balanced view with significant concerns
1585.def: Sevilla views AI as having both great opportunities and serious challenges that require progressive development and careful management.
1585.cite: Me considero en un punto intermedio entre ambas posturas. Es una tecnología con grandes oportunidades y retos. Deberíamos avanzar de forma progresiva.

1585.label: Evidence-based, neutral stance
1585.def: Sevilla sees Epoch's role as providing trustworthy data and evidence about AI to inform policy decisions without pushing for any particular policy agenda.
1585.cite: However, we do not push for any particular stance on AI policy as an organisation. While our staff have their own (diverse) opinions on how AI should be handled, we see Epoch as providing a unique service of informing everyone with trustworthy data and evidence about AI, without pushing for one agenda.

1594.label: Unknown
1594.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1594.label: Catastrophic risk assessment
1594.def: Jesse Clifton views AI risks as potentially involving 'cosmically significant amounts of suffering' (s-risks) and 'catastrophic strategic commitments' by AGI systems.
1594.cite: We at the Center on Long-Term Risk (CLR) are focused on reducing risks of cosmically significant amounts of suffering[1], or s-risks, from transformative artificial intelligence (TAI).

1594.label: Targeted
1594.cite: Submitted as "Targeted" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1597.def: Uses 'Advanced Artificial Intelligence' as effectively the same as 'Transformative AI' (TAI) but intentionally avoids being too prescriptive with definitions.
1597.cite: I'm somewhat loose with definitions here, defining it effectively the same way as "Transformative AI" (TAI) but intentionally not being too prescriptive for the most part (except in cases where definitions become important).

1597.label: Near-term development expected
1597.def: General, highly capable AI systems that will radically upend the existing order.
1597.cite: General, highly capable AI systems are likely to be developed in the next couple of decades, with the possibility of emerging in the next few years.

1597.label: Medium-term development
1597.def: General, highly capable AI systems with greater than 50% chance of development by 2050.
1597.cite: We estimate that there's a high likelihood that general, highly capable AI systems will be developed in the next couple of decades, with a greater than 50% chance of development by 2050.

1597.label: Catastrophic risk assessment
1597.def: AI systems that could cause catastrophic harm through misuse or unpredictable behavior.
1597.cite: Such AI systems will radically upend the existing order - presenting a wide range of risks, scaling up to and including catastrophic threats.

1597.label: Global catastrophic risk
1597.def: Advanced AI systems that could lead to global catastrophes if not properly aligned and safeguarded.
1597.cite: If not properly aligned and safeguarded, advanced AI systems could lead to global catastrophes.

1597.label: Targeted regulation
1597.def: Supports specific AI policies focused on safety and governance, including improving government capacity and advancing technical safety research.
1597.cite: We recommend special attention to funding opportunities to (1) boost global resilience, (2) improve government capacity, (3) coordinate major global players, and (4) advance technical safety research.

1599.label: Unknown
1599.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1599.label: Catastrophic Disruption Concern
1599.def: Big models have potential to cause catastrophic disruption to human societies through misuse and lack of basic legal infrastructure
1599.cite: My concerns about big models are around the potential to cause catastrophic disruption to human societies. I don't really worry about rogue AI. I worry about misuse, but in the same way that we should just say, "oh, if there are terrorists or criminals, and they have a new tool and we need new systems to respond to that".

1599.label: Catastrophic Risk Recognition
1599.def: AI risks include large-scale social harms, malicious uses, and irreversible loss of human control over autonomous systems
1599.cite: AI is progressing rapidly, and companies are shifting their focus to capabilities and autonomy may soon massively amplify AI's impact, with risks that include large-scale social harms, malicious uses, and an irreversible loss of human control over autonomous AI systems.

1599.label: Regulatory Markets
1599.def: Regulatory markets envision licensed private regulatory companies achieving publicly-set regulatory targets through innovation of new regulatory methods
1599.cite: Together with Jack Clark, I've developed a proposal for how we recruit private sector efforts and investment to the challenge of devising more effective and responsive methods for aligning AI systems with democratically-set goals and principles. This approach, which we call regulatory markets, envisions the development of a robust sector of licensed private regulatory companies (profit and non-profit) that achieve publicly-set regulatory targets through innovation of new regulatory methods.

1599.label: IVO Framework
1599.def: Independent Verification Organizations are licensed by government according to their ability to achieve outcomes set by government, selling services to AI developers and deployers
1599.cite: We've got independent verification organizations, they are licensed by government according to their ability to achieve the outcomes set by government, and they are selling services to AI developers and deployers.

1605.label: Near-term concerns
1605.def: Acknowledges that AI timelines are shortening, prompting reconsideration of alternative approaches like brain emulation.
1605.cite: Because as AI timelines are falling, there's also something to be said for the fact that maybe we should revisit the whole brain emulation roadmap that was written in 2007.

1605.label: 5-10 years
1605.def: Views AGI as approaching within a timeframe that requires preparation 5-10 years in advance for coordination strategies.
1605.cite: Those would be valuable factors in creating plans today that, if put in place 5-10 years in advance of a potential power struggle around AGI, could shape the course of action toward coordination and bend the curve toward better global outcomes.

1605.label: Serious risks
1605.def: Views AI risks as serious enough to warrant safety research and coordination efforts, while maintaining tech-optimistic outlook.
1605.cite: I don't just mean, let's push for them as fast as possible, but I think they could alleviate a lot of the existing pain points that we really feel in society right now.

1605.label: Light-touch regulation
1605.def: Favors a tech-optimistic approach that speeds up safety and security-enhancing technologies rather than general regulation.
1605.cite: I think what we can contribute really well is this more differential technology development approach to technology development, which is the term introduced by Bostrom a long, long time ago, but never really got picked up until recently. It basically means you can have a tech-driven approach where you speed up safety and security-enhancing technologies rather than just generally accelerating technologies.

1605.label: Targeted safety measures
1605.def: Supports using cryptographic technologies and security frameworks to prove compliance with safety benchmarks without sharing proprietary information.
1605.cite: You can possibly use cryptographic technologies to prove that you're following certain safety benchmarks or standards. Rather than having to share all of the information that is possibly proprietary, you can only share information that is needed to prove you're hitting certain safety benchmarks.

1607.cite: Submitted as "Restrictive" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1616.label: 10-25 years
1616.cite: Submitted as "10-25 years" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1616.label: Low-medium probability but should focus on due to tractability
1616.cite: I have maybe kind of interestingly mixed view where I think in terms of the likelihood of different harms caused by AI, I think that there's a number that I find reasonably likely in the sense that I would perhaps give them more than 10% chance... And then compared to some of these other longer term concerns, I don't assign as high of a probability to, let's say, specifically catastrophic risks from unsafe AI systems.

1616.label: Focus on lasting impacts and advanced systems
1616.cite: Right, so the way that GovAI tends to frame our focus is we're focused on the lasting impacts of artificial intelligence... I think maybe more the difference is basically how much are you looking at risks caused by systems which are more advanced than the systems that exist today versus purely looking at the harms or risks by the systems that exist right now as maybe the more interesting, significant differential.

1618.label: Serious future risks requiring anticipation
1618.cite: While current narrow AI systems already have significant impacts in specific domains, advanced AI systems could fundamentally transform life through their potential for recursive self-improvement and general problem-solving capabilities, making their development and governance a uniquely critical challenge for humanity's future.

1618.label: Serious risks requiring urgent attention
1618.cite: AI also poses substantial risks both from unintended consequences and from deliberate misuse by malicious actors. Addressing the social and political challenges posed by advanced AI is quickly becoming one of the world's most urgent problems.

1618.label: Support compute-based governance mechanisms
1618.def: Training-compute reporting and thresholds refers to using computational requirements (like FLOPs thresholds) as triggers for AI governance and oversight requirements.
1618.cite: We argue that compute providers should have legal obligations and ethical responsibilities associated with AI development and deployment, both to provide secure infrastructure and to serve as intermediaries for AI regulation.

1618.label: Mixed/conditional support for controls
1618.def: AI chip and model export controls to adversaries refers to restricting the export of advanced AI chips and model weights to countries of concern for national security purposes.
1618.cite: US and Chinese governments should strengthen existing intergovernmental dialogue, covering issues related to national security, such as evaluating models for 'dangerous capabilities' and preventing proliferation to non-state actors.

1618.label: Support liability regimes for AI developers
1618.def: Developer liability for AI-caused harm refers to holding AI developers legally responsible for damages caused by their systems through strict or product liability frameworks.
1618.cite: This approach represents the extension of a standards, licensing, and liability regime to the global level.

1618.label: Support structured access over open release
1618.def: Restrictions on open-weight frontier models refers to limiting the open release of powerful AI model weights above certain capability thresholds, potentially through structured access mechanisms instead.
1618.cite: Recent releases of frontier artificial intelligence (AI) models have largely been gated, providing the benefit of limiting the proliferation of increasingly powerful dual-use capabilities.

1618.label: Support mandatory pre-deployment evaluations
1618.def: Mandatory pre-deployment evaluations refers to requiring safety testing, capability evaluations, and risk assessments as a condition for releasing AI systems.
1618.cite: On the basis of advanced AI's risk profile, the sensitivity of information involved in the auditing process, and the high costs of verifying safety and benefit claims of AI Labs, we recommend that public bodies become directly involved in safety critical, especially gray- and white-box, AI model evaluations.

1618.label: Targeted governance
1618.cite: The work will include understanding the form and frameworks of national and international AI regulation, exploring the technological feasibility of using AI and machine learning technologies themselves to facilitate the governance of AI, and investigating how the AI industry can and should cooperate with international institutions for public benefit.

1618.label: Targeted international coordination
1618.cite: We find strong benefits of internationalisation in compute-provider oversight, content provenance, model evaluations, incident monitoring, and risk management protocols. In contrast, the benefits of internationalisation are lower or mixed in data privacy, data provenance, chip distribution, and bias mitigation.

1618.label: Oppose federal preemption
1618.def: Federal preemption refers to preventing states from creating their own AI oversight standards and regulations through federal legislation that blocks enforcement of state and local AI laws.
1618.cite: We propose that states establish an International AI Organization (IAIO) to certify state jurisdictions (not firms or AI projects) for compliance with international oversight standards.

1626.label: Questioning traditional definitions
1626.def: AGI traditionally meant machines that think, learn, and adapt like humans, but questions whether this anthropomorphic view makes sense given our limited understanding of human intelligence itself.
1626.cite: The term AGI made sense from far away, when AI systems were very narrow. Now we have systems that are quite general already and the term does not mean as much.

1626.label: Near-term AGI
1626.def: AGI as systems capable of performing any intellectual task a human can do, including replacing any intellectual human labor like remote work tasks.
1626.cite: I believe AI will continue to improve rapidly and will soon be able to replace any intellectual human labor—think of anything your remote coworker can do.

1626.label: Significant risks requiring management
1626.cite: By the way, I am not a big fan of the "killer robot" meme. I don't think that this is a realistic scenario of how AI catastrophe would unfold.

1626.label: Risk-focused regulation
1626.cite: AI is a technology that moves so rapidly that governance processes regulating frontier AI models must be informed by state-of-the-art research.

1628.label: Serious risk
1628.cite: SecureBio has shown that leading AIs can provide practical assistance on tasks that can enable bioweapon development. Leading models now outperform PhD researchers in several such areas.

1628.label: Catastrophic risk
1628.cite: Their motivation is simple but vital: to help ensure AI contributes to a future of profound scientific and human progress, while reducing the risk of catastrophic misuse of these models.

1628.label: Oppose compute thresholds
1628.def: Compute thresholds use training compute power as a proxy for risk to trigger regulatory requirements.
1628.cite: Compute thresholds are a poor proxy for risk and their efficacy could break down with the introduction of specialized models that are highly capable but require relatively little pre-training compute.

1628.label: Support tighter controls
1628.def: AI chip export controls to adversaries involve restrictions on advanced computing semiconductors and model weights to prevent military applications.
1628.cite: The team also presented to export-control policymakers through the BIS Technology Advisory Committee and briefed US CAISI staff working on bio-related standards, both efforts to translate technical work into governance-ready inputs.

1628.label: Strongly support mandatory testing
1628.def: Pre-deployment evaluations involve rigorous testing of frontier AI models before release to identify harmful capabilities.
1628.cite: Given evidence that certain AI systems pose national security risks, testing and evaluation mandates for frontier models are essential to mitigate such risks.

1628.label: Targeted regulation
1628.cite: Expert-level AI assistance for virology should be regulated as a dual-use technology, subject to similar controls as physical biological materials and equipment.

1629.label: Serious risks requiring scientific approach
1629.def: AI risks are significant enough to warrant dedicated scientific research and mitigation across multiple domains including public safety, national security, and potential for adversarial misuse.
1629.cite: Chem-bio research: Identifying paths to advance the science of evaluating chemical-biological misuse risks of foundation models with key takeaways in three areas: capability assessment, threat modeling, and evaluation methodology.

1629.label: Targeted regulation
1629.def: Science-based approach to AI safety focusing on specific risk areas including generative AI risk management, synthetic content, evaluations, red-teaming, and model safety and security through voluntary standards and collaborative research.
1629.cite: Voluntary reporting approaches: Key findings on how organizations can share risk management data and analysis through a voluntary reporting template (VRT) for the NIST AI Risk Management Framework Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1).

1630.label: Unknown
1630.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1630.label: Serious risks
1630.cite: we are at risk of further entrenching the dominance of a handful of private actors over our economy and our social institutions. Concentration of power is a result of the accumulation of data, compute, talent, and capital within a small number of companies who have reaped the rewards of the surveillance business model.

1630.label: Serious risks
1630.cite: Everybody's talking about futuristic risks, the singularity, existential risk. They're distracting from the fact that the thing that really scares these companies is regulation. Regulation today.

1630.label: Moderate regulation
1630.cite: A federal data privacy law, especially one with strong data minimization, could challenge the culture of impunity and recklessness in the AI industry that's already hurting both consumers and competition. If there's any single point I want to make today: it's that now is the moment where passing such a law matters most: before the trajectory has been set.

1630.label: Moderate regulation
1630.cite: this voluntary consensus between companies is fragile and it will continue to be fragile until it is baked into national regulatory frameworks by lawmakers that are accountable to the public.

1660.label: Powerful enough vs True AGI distinction
1660.def: There already are general AIs that are not powerful enough yet to count as True AGIs
1660.cite: The Race for powerful AGIs has already started. There already are general AIs. They just are not powerful enough yet to count as True AGIs.

1660.label: Less than 5 years significant probability
1660.def: AGI happening soon with significant probability in less than 5 years
1660.cite: AGI is happening soon. Significant probability of it happening in less than 5 years.

1660.label: Human extinction risk
1660.def: AI poses significant risk of human extinction if not controlled
1660.cite: AI poses an extinction risk on par with nuclear war.

1660.label: Prevent development for 20 years
1660.def: Implementing regulatory measures to prevent artificial superintelligence development for at least 20 years
1660.cite: The most urgent priority is to prevent the development of artificial superintelligence for the next 20 years.

1660.label: Global ban advocate
1660.def: A global ban on superintelligent AI systems that can outsmart and overpower humans
1660.cite: We should ban the development of superintelligent AI to prevent potential human extinction.

1662.label: Functional definition
1662.def: AGI is defined as when AI can do all tasks a human can on a computer and conduct AI research itself.
1662.cite: At the point when AI is able to do all of the tasks a human can on a computer, it will functionally be AGI and able to conduct the same AI research that we can.

1662.label: Medium-term timeline
1662.def: Adam Shimi views AGI timeline research as fraught with epistemological mistakes including arbitrary guesses and piled assumptions leading to uninformative results.
1662.cite: What this post does is uncover the main epistemological mistakes made by almost everyone trying their hands at figuring out timelines. Among others, there is: * Taking arbitrary guesses within a set of options that you don't have enough evidence to separate * Piling on arbitrary assumption on arbitraty assumption, leading to completely uninforma...(read more)

1662.label: Existential risk
1662.def: Adam Shimi co-authored The Compendium arguing that AGI poses extinction risk for humanity because we will be powerless to control superintelligent systems as they reshape the world.
1662.cite: these systems pose an extinction risk for humanity not because they are malicious, but because we will be powerless to control them as they reshape the world, indifferent to our fate.

1662.label: Serious concern
1662.def: Adam Shimi considers multipolar failure scenarios as among the most important posts on AI risk, highlighting structural risks that don't require agency.
1662.cite: I consider this post as one of the most important ever written on issues of timelines and AI doom scenario. Not because it's perfect (some of its assumptions are unconvincing), but because it highlights a key aspect of AI Risk and the alignment problem which is so easy to miss coming from a rationalist mindset: it doesn't require an agent to take over the whole world. It is not about agency.

1662.label: Restrictive
1662.cite: Submitted as "Restrictive" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1663.label: Median 5-7 years for autonomous capabilities
1663.def: Powerful AI that can autonomously outcompete well-run human-plus-AI organizations, with median timeline around 14 years
1663.cite: My median for that is probably five to seven years, at least if we're talking about actually doing this to a high standard, along the lines of the best human penetration testers. My median is probably more like 14 years.

1663.label: Manageable with proper planning
1663.cite: Adam believes that a well-implemented defense-in-depth approach to AI safety has a pretty good chance of working. Adam nevertheless makes the case that with proper planning, meticulous experimental design, and at least some willingness to accept performance trade-offs when necessary, the plan can work.

1663.label: Catastrophic risks requiring defense-in-depth
1663.cite: They released this public statement warning that advanced AI systems could pose a risk similar to that of nuclear war or widespread pandemics.

1663.label: Engineering standards approach
1663.cite: What I think we should really be focusing on as a community is developing a standard for safe AI that meets three criteria. The first and maybe key one is assurance: if something meets the standard, it's actually safe with a high level of probability.

1676.label: Unknown
1676.cite: Submitted as "Unknown" via crowdsourced form (evidence: Explicitly stated)

1676.label: Existential catastrophe risk
1676.cite: We argue that this dynamic could lead to an effectively irreversible loss of human influence over crucial societal systems, precipitating an existential catastrophe through the permanent disempowerment of humanity.

1676.label: Existential risk
1676.cite: Podle mě je nejhorším rizikem pozvolná ztráta lidského vlivu na civilizaci. Na druhém místě je ztráta kontroly, jak ji popisuje studie AI 2027. A na třetím místě je zneužití lidmi.

1676.label: Restrictive regulation
1676.cite: Podle mě je nutná. Pokud to má dopadnout dobře, tak AI jako technologii regulovat musíme.

1690.def: AGI systems with superhuman labour abilities, generality across all human tasks, and agency to plan and execute their own objectives.
1690.cite: In order for the price of some labour to fall to zero, AI systems will have to reach a point of generality - meaning the AI can do any task that a human can do but to a superhuman level. Some call this artificial general intelligence (AGI).

That prediction, I suspect, is too cautious. Once AI is able to think for itself and program itself, the concept of measuring progress in years and decades will be hopelessly outdated. We could well pass this point of 'intelligence explosion' in 2026.

1690.label: Precautionary
1690.cite: But, as I say, there's a simple cure to avoid this dystopia. We urgently need to implement safety checks before we release AI models.

1690.label: Restrictive
1690.cite: The UK Government should begin by 1) investing in AI monitoring technology to track progress and risks, 2) to focus on more and subsidise AI safety efforts rather than just AI capability work, and 3) lead the world with an effective and fair regulatory framework to inhibit corner-cutting when it comes to the safety aspects of AI.

1696.label: Oppose strict developer liability
1696.def: Developer liability frameworks that impose strict liability on AI labs for harms caused by their systems, particularly when users misuse outputs or override safety measures.
1696.cite: The core mechanism is a negligence floor. Under the proposed language, an AI developer is not liable unless a plaintiff can demonstrate that the lab knew — or should have known — of a specific, foreseeable harm and failed to act. That is a materially higher bar than the product-liability standards that plaintiffs' attorneys have been testing in early AI tort cases.

1696.label: Support preemption
1696.def: Federal preemption means Congress establishing a federal AI framework that displaces conflicting state laws to create uniform national standards.
1696.cite: Congress must lead. Although federal preemption language may not be perfect, it's far better than the regulatory fragmentation we will face if Congress fails to act. The stakes are too high, the technology too important and the competitive landscape too fierce to let a regulatory race determine America's AI future.

1698.label: Opposes compute-based regulatory approaches
1698.def: Ex ante rules that dictate how firms compete in the marketplace or how markets are structured based on computational thresholds.
1698.cite: We are loathe to create ex ante rules that dictate how firms compete in the marketplace or how markets are structured. If we try to impose our beliefs about how the AI market should look—about how many competitors there should be or how much they should charge for their products or what features the products should have—we are more likely to dampen innovation.

1698.label: Skeptical of restrictions on open weights

Along with enacting regulations requiring companies to test AI the largest, most powerful models before releasing them, this bill carves out a space for public benefit in the field of AI.

1762.label: Serious risks
1762.def: AI poses significant economic and social risks including market manipulation, privacy violations, and concentrated corporate power that require immediate policy intervention.
1762.cite: The current guardrails that could keep AI tools from raising prices and protect against concentrated economic power and anticompetitive corporate behavior are inadequate. Intense tech industry lobbying efforts to stop policymaking and enforcement—coupled with underresourced regulators and the inherently opaque systems that power these tools—have resulted in an anemic governmental response to these cost-driving technologies.

1762.label: Strongly support compute thresholds
1762.def: Compute-based thresholds for frontier models trained with more than 10^26 computing operations to trigger safety and transparency requirements.
1762.cite: SB 53 takes a targeted approach, applying its requirements only to frontier models (trained with more than 10^26 computing operations) developed by organizations with $500 million or more in annual revenue, concentrating regulatory attention on the entities most capable of producing potentially dangerous AI systems while avoiding burdens on smaller developers, academic researchers, and open-source projects.

1762.label: Strongly support mandatory testing

1762.cite: With AI advancing rapidly and new models demonstrating remarkable abilities, the proposed legislation sets clear standards for developers of the biggest, untested, next-generation AI models. It would require developers of these models to take basic precautions such as pre-deployment safety testing and cybersecurity protections, and includes new state enforcement mechanisms.

1762.label: Moderate regulation
1762.def: Multi-faceted regulatory approach including safety standards, transparency requirements, antitrust enforcement, and public infrastructure investment to address AI's economic and social impacts.
1762.cite: California can course correct. The state can and must act swiftly to rein in AI's impact on distorted markets and runaway prices. California's leaders must take bold action to ensure competition, fair markets, and consumer choice, ultimately making the state an affordable place to live.

1762.label: Moderate regulation
1762.def: Support for safety and transparency requirements for AI companies while promoting public infrastructure and democratized access.
1762.cite: The AI market is dominated by a handful of corporate actors and this essential legislation takes the first critical step in fostering greater innovation and openness to serve the public interest. With the development of a public cloud like CalCompute, we can harness the potential of AI for good, accessing data to advance the next breakthroughs in healthcare, technology, and disaster response.

1762.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1762.def: Federal preemption would block enforcement of all state and local legislation governing AI systems for a full decade.

1762.label: Strongly oppose federal preemption
1762.def: Federal preemption would strip states like California of their right to institute common-sense AI guardrails and remove state authority to regulate AI.
1762.cite: That's why we've joined over 30 California-based organizations—including Common Sense Media, PowerSwitch Action, SEIU California, California Federation of Labor Unions, Center for AI and Digital Policy, and the Western Center on Law and Poverty—in a letter to Governor Gavin Newsom, Attorney General Rob Bonta and our California legislative leaders urging them to publicly oppose any attempt to force AI preemption into federal legislation and unilaterally remove our state's authority through Executive Order.

1772.def: AGI as the intersection of three key properties: high Autonomy (independence of action), high Generality (broad scope and adaptability), and high Intelligence (competence at cognitive tasks), combined at levels matching or exceeding top human capability.

1772.label: Very short timelines
1772.def: AGI as systems with high autonomy, broad generality, and superior intelligence that can match or exceed top human capability across cognitive tasks.
1772.cite: AI corporations and their leaders publicly and privately believe that AGI (by some definition) is achievable within a few years. Expert predictors with proven track-records assign 25% probability to AGI (by some definition) arriving within 1-2 years, and 50% for 2-5 years.

1772.def: AGI and superintelligence pose risks ranging from huge disruption of society to permanent human disempowerment or extinction.

1772.label: Catastrophic and existential risks
1772.def: Advanced AI systems present catastrophic and even existential risks that could emerge due to misuse, unintended consequences, or misalignment.
1772.cite: Advanced AI systems have the potential to exacerbate current harms such as discrimination and disinformation, and present catastrophic and even existential risks going forward.

1772.label: Restrictive regulation
1772.def: A coordinated set of governance measures including hard caps on AI computation, enhanced liability for dangerous AI systems, and tiered regulation with extensive safety guarantees required before development and deployment of the most capable systems.

Second, we should implement hard caps on AI computation... Third, we need enhanced liability for the most dangerous AI systems... Fourth, we need tiered regulation based on risk levels.

1772.label: Pause AGI development
1772.def: A mandatory pause on development of AI more powerful than GPT-4 with licensing requirements for powerful AI systems.
1772.cite: We still call for a pause. We still think we should pause AGI development now until we have a much better system in place for managing the risks of it.

1772.label: Immediate regulation needed
1772.def: Immediate implementation of licensing, registration of computational resources, rigorous auditing, and clear red lines about intolerable risks.
1772.cite: We implore Congress to immediately regulate these systems before they cause irreparable damage, and provide five principles for effective oversight.
